context: in the text in verse 29 in Acts 20 that there is external danger. There's external danger. The Bible says very clearly that he says, I know this, that after my departing shall grievous wolves enter in among you, not sparing the flock.

context: We read in Acts 20 in verse 28 already, where the Bible says that he purchased it, the church, with his own blood.

context: Peter could stand up with great boldness in the book of Acts in the fourth chapter in verse number 12 and say, there's no other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved.

context: The Bible says that he sees this bright light and he hears a voice. And the voice is, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?
point_summary: The Damascus Road encounter with Christ transformed Saul from self-directed religious authority to one who immediately submitted his will to the lordship of Jesus, asking "what wilt thou have me to do?"

context: And we find that Saul answers and says, who art thou, Lord? I am Jesus, whom thou persecutest. It is hard for thee to kick against the pricks.
point_summary: Saul's immediate response to Christ on the Damascus road—"Lord, what wilt thou have me to do?"—illustrates that genuine salvation transfers authority over one's life to Jesus Christ.

context: God is no respecter of persons
point_summary: God's impartiality—drawn from Acts 10:34—grounds the assurance that the same comfort and sufficient grace Paul experienced is equally available to every believer, without exception.

context: Peter looks at him and says, look over here, and I think the man had every expectation that he was going to get some type of monetary gift. And then those words come out of Peter's mouth, silver and gold have I none.
point_summary: The miracle Peter performed at the temple gate illustrates that time spent with God produces a visible, undeniable divine presence that others recognize and that accomplishes far more than human resources alone can offer.

context: And the Bible tells us that he gets up, man, he's leaping and praising God. He goes into the temple.
point_summary: Spending time with God produces the kind of visible, miraculous fruit—illustrated by the lame man leaping in Acts 3—that causes others to recognize God's presence in a believer's life.

context: And he says this, and there's no other name under heaven given among men whereby ye must be saved.
point_summary: Salvation belongs exclusively to the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth — a truth Peter proclaimed boldly before hostile religious authorities who could not silence it.

context: If you read the very next verse, I believe it's Acts 4.13, it says that they perceived that they were ignorant and unlearned men. But that they had been with Jesus.
point_summary: The observable boldness of Peter and John—ordinary, unlearned men—was attributed by their opponents not to natural ability but to their having been with Jesus, a testimony the sermon connects to Abimelech's recognition of God's presence with Abraham.

context: after they finished the first missionary journey, if you were to go back in Acts chapter number 14 and see how they circled back to each of those cities
point_summary: Acts 14 is cited to illustrate that Paul, after his first missionary journey, returned to each city to encourage new believers to continue in their faith and established local leadership to sustain those decisions.

context: He says that in Acts 20 and verse number 24, but none of these things move me, neither count on my life dear unto myself, that I might finish my course with joy.
point_summary: Paul's desire to finish his course with joy (Acts 20:24) is presented as the model for a spiritual leader's longing that those he ministers to would likewise endure and finish faithfully.

context: Paul, as his manner was in verse 2 of Acts 17, says that he went in unto them and three Sabbath days reasoned with them out of the Scriptures.
point_summary: Paul's customary practice of entering synagogues and reasoning from the Scriptures over three Sabbaths is cited as the pattern by which he argued that Jesus is the Christ—connecting the name Jesus to the title Christ for a Jewish audience already familiar with the historical figure.

context: Notice in verse number 3 of Acts 17, opening and alleging that Christ must needs have suffered and risen again from the dead, and that this Jesus whom I preach unto you is Christ.
point_summary: Paul's proclamation in the synagogue—"this Jesus whom I preach unto you is Christ"—is cited to show that identifying Jesus as the Messiah was the apostolic method of preaching to those who knew the historical figure but needed the theological truth declared.

context: Remember when the Apostle Paul, his name was Saul at the time of his conversion in Acts chapter 9, who art thou Lord? And then he says, Lord what wilt thou have me to do?
point_summary: Paul's conversion cry in Acts 9—"Who art thou, Lord?"—is cited as evidence that recognizing Jesus as the Christ naturally issues in submission and worship.

context: after Jesus tells Paul Saul at the time Who he was I am Jesus whom thou persecutest that Paul Saul at the time Responds by saying Lord What will thou have me to do?
point_summary: Paul's immediate response at his conversion—"Lord, what wilt thou have me to do?"—is presented as the defining moment of his enlistment into ministry, the question every believer should ask upon salvation.

context: when he is sharing his testimony in Acts chapter number 22 He says that Jesus said this to him and it's recorded in Acts 22 in verse number 21 He says depart for I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles
point_summary: Paul's divine enlistment into ministry is grounded in Christ's direct commission at conversion: "Depart, for I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles."

context: at midday when they were on their way to Damascus Oh King I saw on the way a light from heaven above the brightness of the Sun Shining round about me and then which journeyed with me
point_summary: Paul's Damascus Road encounter—seeing a light brighter than the sun and hearing Christ's voice—is cited as the divine call that commissioned him specifically to minister to the Gentiles.

context: when we were all fallen to the earth I heard a voice speaking unto me and saying in the Hebrew tongue Saul Saul
point_summary: Paul's conversion on the Damascus road, including the voice from heaven recorded in Acts 26:14, establishes the divine origin of his apostolic call and explains why he understood himself as sent specifically to the Gentiles.

context: Why persecuted thou me it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks and I said who art thou? Lord, and he said, I am Jesus whom thou persecutest.
point_summary: Paul's encounter on the Damascus road, recorded in Acts 9, is the foundational event establishing that his ministry was by divine call, not human appointment.

context: In Acts 9 is the record of this immediate event and that's where verse number 6 tells us that Saul then says, Lord what wilt thou have me to do?
point_summary: Paul's surrender on the Damascus road — "Lord, what wilt thou have me to do?" — marks the moment his divine call to ministry began, which Acts 26:16 then defines as being made a minister and witness.

context: Notice in verse 16 of Acts 26, but rise and stand upon thy feet, for I have appeared unto thee for this purpose.
point_summary: Paul's divine appointment at his conversion—commissioned as "a minister and a witness"—establishes that his ministry to the Gentiles was not self-chosen but directly mandated by Christ.

context: Notice this phrase, to make thee a minister and a witness, both of these things which thou hast seen and of those things in the which I will appear unto thee, delivering thee from the people and from the Gentiles unto whom now I send thee,
point_summary: Paul's divine commission to the Gentiles—delivering, sending, and opening eyes—establishes that his ministry in Colossians 1 was not self-appointed but directly called by Christ.

context: to open their eyes and to turn them from darkness to light and from the power of Satan unto God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and inheritance among them which are sanctified by faith that is in me.
point_summary: Paul's divine commission—to open eyes, turn people from darkness to light, and from Satan's power to God—is cited as the basis for his understanding that he was enlisted into ministry by God's direct call.

context: after the disciples were beaten and sent out and said, 'Don't'
point_summary: Suffering for Christ's name is met with rejoicing, as the beaten disciples' response in Acts 5:41 illustrates the same perspective Paul commends to the Colossians.

context: There's no other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved.
point_summary: Epaphras's example in Colossians 4:12 is cited to illustrate the consistent goal of presenting every person perfect in Christ, echoing Paul's stated purpose in Colossians 1:28.

context: He said to his disciples before he would ascend to heaven that they should receive power after the Holy Ghost has come upon you. Then you shall be witnesses unto me both in Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, the uttermost part of the earth.
point_summary: The power promised in Acts 1:8 is cited to show that Spirit-given enablement, not personal strength, is the necessary foundation for fulfilling the ministry to which believers are called.

context: Stephen, the first martyr there in Acts chapter number seven, as he's being stoned to death, he prays that it would not be laid to their charge.
point_summary: Stephen's dying prayer that his stoning not be charged to his killers is cited alongside Paul's words and Christ's cross-prayer as parallel examples that believers can forgive rather than grow bitter under opposition.

context: Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?
point_summary: Paul's conversion on the Damascus road—where the risen Christ stopped him with the words "Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?"—is presented as the foundational moment of salvation and immediate surrender that qualified him for God's service.

context: Who art thou, Lord?
point_summary: Saving grace, not religious zeal, is the foundation of all genuine service — as Paul's Damascus Road encounter, where Saul was saved and surrendered in the same moment, demonstrates.

context: What will thou have me to do?
point_summary: Genuine service to God begins only at salvation, illustrated by Paul's Damascus Road encounter where being saved and surrendered to Christ's lordship occurred simultaneously.

context: Acts 1 and verse 8, but ye shall receive power. Oh, there's that word again. We already talked about power. Oh, wait a minute. Ye shall receive power after that the Holy Ghost has come upon you and ye shall be my witnesses unto me, both at the same time in Jerusalem.
point_summary: The power promised in Acts 1:8 is the Spirit's enabling for witness in hostile territory—the very city where Christ was crucified and his enemies remained.

context: We find that the disciples there in Acts chapter number one, when they saw the ascension
point_summary: The certainty of Christ's return, confirmed by the angelic promise at the ascension, is presented as a primary motivation for pressing on in faithful witness until He comes.

context: in verse number 10 of Acts 5, that she fell down straight with his feet and yielded up the ghost and the young men came and found her dead and carrying her forth buried her by her husband and great fear came upon all the church and upon as many as heard these things.
point_summary: The sudden deaths of Ananias and Sapphira are cited as a biblical illustration that God's consuming holiness produces the reverent, godly fear with which believers are called to serve him acceptably.

context: You'll see that in Acts 17 and verse number 29.
point_summary: The biblical term "Godhead," found in Acts 17:29, provides the scriptural vocabulary underlying the doctrine of the Trinity, even though the word "Trinity" itself does not appear in the Bible.

context: Peter is given insight here. The Holy Spirit gives truth to Peter with regard to the portion that was kept back.
point_summary: Lying to the Holy Spirit (v. 3) and lying to God (v. 4) are equated without contradiction, demonstrating that the Holy Spirit is God and thus a member of the Trinity.

context: They bring these things to the apostles' feet, and they act as if they had given everything.
point_summary: Lying to the Holy Spirit (v. 3) and lying to God (v. 4) are equated without contradiction, demonstrating that the Holy Spirit is God.

context: Peter says to Ananias in verse 3 of Acts 5, why hath Satan filled thine heart to lie to the Holy Ghost?
point_summary: The equation of lying to the Holy Ghost (v. 3) with lying to God (v. 4) is presented as Peter's explicit acknowledgment that the Holy Spirit is God.

context: And then in verse 4, at the end of the verse, he says, thou hast not lied unto men, but unto God.
point_summary: Peter's parallel statements in Acts 5:3–4—lying to the Holy Ghost is lying to God—are cited as direct scriptural acknowledgment that the Holy Spirit is God.

context: Turn back to Acts chapter 20, if you will, and I want you to see what happens in Acts chapter 20 that really gives us some insight.
point_summary: Acts 20 is referenced to establish the historical context that Paul addressed the Ephesian church elders directly, providing scriptural background for why Timothy's charge to guard sound doctrine in Ephesus carries such weight.

context: Paul is speaking to the church leaders of the church at Ephesus.
point_summary: Acts 20:17 is cited to establish the scriptural backdrop that Paul addressed the Ephesian church leaders, providing interpretive context for why Timothy was stationed there to guard sound doctrine.

context: Paul is finishing up his interaction with the leaders of the church at Ephesus, and he's concluding this message to them, and he's speaking to them about the importance of his ministry there and then charging them.
point_summary: Foreknowledge of false teachers infiltrating Ephesus after Paul's departure establishes the divine rationale for placing Timothy there to guard sound doctrine.

context: Take heed therefore unto yourselves and to all the flock over the which the Holy Ghost hath made you overseers, to feed the church of God which he hath purchased with his own blood.
point_summary: Elders appointed by the Holy Spirit as overseers are charged to feed the church of God through spiritual nourishment—the proclamation of sound doctrine—as the divinely ordained remedy against false teachers.

context: to the church leaders at Ephesus in Acts chapter number 20
point_summary: Paul's warning in Acts 20 that false teachers would arise from within the Ephesian church itself is the divine foreknowledge that made Timothy's appointment there a deliberate, answering provision in God's plan.

context: there is no other name given under given among men under heaven given among men Whereby we must be saved Peter is speaking in Acts 4 about the name of Jesus
point_summary: Denying the person of Jesus Christ is the defining error of antichrists and false teachers, because Acts 4:12 establishes that salvation exists in no other name.

context: when those that are Opposing the work of the ministry say this ye have filled Jerusalem with your doctrine
point_summary: The accusation of Christ's opponents in Acts 5:28 — "ye have filled Jerusalem with your doctrine" — is cited as evidence that disciples' obedience to the Great Commission produced gospel saturation beyond measure.

context: When Paul and Silas would arrive to the city of Thessalonica that those in opposition said these That have turned the world upside down Are come hither
point_summary: Acts 17:6, cited alongside Acts 5:28, illustrates through the words of the gospel's own opponents that faithful obedience to the Great Commission results in world-transforming, measurable gospel advance.

context: Also these that have turned the world upside down have come hither also that's Acts 17 in verse number 6
point_summary: The gospel's transformative power is so world-altering that even its opponents in Thessalonica were compelled to acknowledge it, declaring that those preaching it had "turned the world upside down."

context: that second statement that's made in the book of Acts that's so impactful was in Acts 17 in verse six when Paul and Silas arrived at the city of Thessalonica
point_summary: The gospel is a divine trust—a privilege granted by God—not a platform for self-promotion, as Paul's ministry at Thessalonica and his letter back to those believers together demonstrate.

context: ...Paul was converted and it's recorded for us in Acts chapter number nine on the road to Damascus...
point_summary: Paul's Damascus Road conversion in Acts 9 is cited as the starting point of his gospel preparation, leading into Galatians 1 to show how that readiness was developed before his missionary journeys.

context: this is when they arrive in Thessalonica, according to verse number one
point_summary: Paul's arrival in Thessalonica (Acts 17:1) serves as the narrative setting for verse 2, where his customary practice of reasoning from the Scriptures illustrates the consistent, habitual passion driving his gospel ministry.

context: notice in verse two, and Paul, look at these words, as his manner was, went in unto them in three Sabbath days, reasoned with them out of the Scriptures
point_summary: Paul's unashamed gospel proclamation is grounded in habitual practice: Acts 17:2 is cited to show that reasoning from Scripture was Paul's consistent manner wherever he went.

context: Now while Paul waited for them in Athens, his spirit was stirred in him when he saw the city wholly given to idolatry.
point_summary: Paul's spirit being "stirred" by Athens's idolatry illustrates that genuine gospel passion moves beyond emotion to action, a pattern the sermon holds up as the standard for believers today.

context: as I passed by and beheld your devotions, I found an altar with this inscription to the unknown God, whom therefore ye ignorantly worship, him declare I unto you
point_summary: Paul's declaration to the Athenians—"whom therefore ye ignorantly worship, him declare I unto you"—is cited as the model response to a lost world: name the unknown God rather than shrink from criticism.

context: and he reasoned in the synagogue every Sabbath and persuaded the Jews and the Greeks
point_summary: Paul's passionate, consistent reasoning in the synagogue every Sabbath at Corinth illustrates the gospel-driven urgency that compels proclamation to both Jews and Greeks despite opposition.

context: then spake the Lord to Paul in the night by a vision, be not afraid but speak and hold not thy peace
point_summary: Amid fierce opposition at Corinth, Christ's direct command to Paul—"be not afraid, but speak"—is presented as divine encouragement that fueled Paul's continued gospel proclamation.

context: God says the Lord says be not afraid but speak and hold not thy peace. Be not afraid and speak. He says I am with thee.
point_summary: Christ's direct command to Paul — "be not afraid but speak" — paired with the promise "I am with thee," is presented as divine encouragement that sustained gospel proclamation in the face of opposition at Corinth.

context: Notice what he says in verse 20 and 21. And how I kept back nothing that was profitable unto you but have showed you and have taught you publicly and from house to house
point_summary: Paul's passion for the gospel is seen in his declaration that he withheld nothing profitable, teaching publicly and house to house, testifying repentance and faith to both Jews and Greeks.

context: testifying both to the Jews and also to the Greeks repentance toward God and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Paul's gospel proclamation to both Jews and Greeks—summarized as repentance toward God and faith toward the Lord Jesus Christ—is presented as the content he held nothing back from delivering, driven by tears and passion.

context: Skip down in verse number 31 if you will. Therefore watch and remember that by the space of three years I ceased not to warn everyone of
point_summary: Paul's three-year, night-and-day ministry of warning everyone with tears illustrates the depth of passion that understanding the gospel's stakes produces in a faithful preacher.

context: Acts chapter number four records a very interesting passage of scripture
point_summary: Acts 4 is introduced as a passage about the imperative of consistent, regular communion with God for believers.

context: as we look at Acts chapter number four, we find that Peter and John are being questioned by the religious leaders.
point_summary: Acts 4 is introduced as the setting where Peter and John face questioning by religious leaders following the healing at the Beautiful Gate, with the sermon's focus falling specifically on how Peter responds to that interrogation.

context: the miracle that is recorded in Acts chapter number three. No doubting, notable miracle had taken place in Acts chapter number three.
point_summary: The healing of the lame man at the Gate Beautiful in Acts 3 is presented as a God-given opportunity that opened the door for gospel proclamation.

context: Peter speaks to him and the man is gloriously healed. And what a wonderful event that we find recorded there in Acts chapter number three
point_summary: The healing of the lame man at the Gate Beautiful in Acts 3 is cited as the notable miracle that prompted the religious leaders' interrogation of Peter and John in Acts 4, providing context for the sermon's focus on boldness born from being with Jesus.

context: Peter speaks up and delivers a powerful response here in Acts chapter number four
point_summary: The religious leaders' astonished recognition in Acts 4:13 — that Peter and John's boldness was traceable not to formal training but to their having been with Jesus — frames the sermon's call to consistent communion with the Lord.

context: I want you to notice in verse number 13, and we'll kind of catch some context here, but let me just read verse 13
point_summary: Consistent time in communion with Christ—not formal religious training—is what the religious leaders recognized as the source of Peter and John's remarkable boldness.

context: And they took knowledge of them that they had been with Jesus.
point_summary: The religious leaders' recognition that Peter and John had "been with Jesus" — not formal training — was the source of their observable boldness, and this phrase serves as the sermon's governing theme.

context: And as they spake unto the people, the priests and the captain of the temple and the Sadducees came upon them.
point_summary: Religious opposition from the priests, temple captain, and Sadducees arose precisely because Peter and John were preaching the resurrection of Jesus, confirming that faithful gospel proclamation invites resistance.

context: And they laid hands on them and put them in hold until the next day, for it was now eventide.
point_summary: The arrest of Peter and John by the religious authorities is presented as the hostile backdrop against which the gospel nonetheless advanced, with thousands believing even as the apostles were imprisoned.

context: And it says this, and the number of the men was about 5,000.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We often speak about Pentecost and the 3,000 that were added into the church.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: But it says in verse number five, and it came to pass on the morrow, that their rulers and elders and scribes...
point_summary: Acts 4:5 sets the scene of an intimidating assembly of rulers, elders, and high-priestly figures before whom Peter and John are placed, establishing the hostile context that makes Peter's Spirit-filled response all the more remarkable.

context: Jesus had told them before his ascension in Acts chapter number one, but you shall receive power after that the Holy Ghost has come upon you.
point_summary: Acts 1:8's promise of power through the Holy Spirit is fulfilled in Acts 4:8, where Peter's bold response before hostile religious leaders demonstrates what Spirit-filled witness looks like in practice.

context: This is the stone which was set at naught of you builders, which has become the head of the corner.
point_summary: Christ as the rejected stone who has become the cornerstone is cited as the scriptural ground for Peter's declaration that salvation exists in no other name.

context: Neither is there salvation in any other, for there's none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved.
point_summary: Salvation is found exclusively in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth—the crucified and risen one—a claim Peter makes boldly before the very authorities who condemned him.

context: ...what these religious leaders in Acts chapter number 4 said of Peter and John, they took knowledge of them that they had been with Jesus.
point_summary: The religious leaders' observation in Acts 4 that Peter and John "had been with Jesus" illustrates that fellowship with Christ prepares believers for hardship, even when it does not prevent it.

context: ...if this counsel or this work be of men, it will come to naught...
point_summary: Gamaliel's principle—that any work truly of God cannot be overthrown—frames the apostles' subsequent beating and joyful suffering as confirmation of divine origin and prepared endurance.

context: ...if it be of God, ye cannot overthrow it, lest happily ye be found even to fight against God...
point_summary: Even when authorities acknowledge that a work cannot be overthrown if it is of God, persecution may still follow, yet those who daily walk with God are prepared to respond with rejoicing rather than defeat.

context: After they were beaten and after they were threatened, they went out rejoicing that they were counted worthy to suffer shame for His namesake.
point_summary: Joy that sustains believers through suffering—even beatings—flows from fellowship with God, whose presence produces the fullness of joy that makes rejoicing amid hardship possible.

context: when they saw the boldness of Peter and John
point_summary: Boldness before intimidating authorities is a product of fellowship with Christ, evidenced when observers recognized in Peter and John that they had been with Jesus.

context: We've used it as a theme in our church with Missions Conference, boldness to speak thy word. We've used it in theology in verse number 29
point_summary: Boldness to speak God's word is presented as a product of fellowship with God, evidenced by the early church's prayer in Acts 4:29 and the Spirit-empowered proclamation that immediately followed.

context: it's interesting because the Bible says this, it tells us in verse number 24 that when they heard that, they lifted up
point_summary: Prayer lifted to God together (v. 24) is identified as an act of fellowship that produces boldness, as the gathered believers' corporate cry leads directly to Spirit-filled, bold proclamation in v. 31.

context: that account on the road to Damascus as we would read of it in Acts chapter nine.
point_summary: Paul's Damascus Road conversion in Acts 9 is cited as the experiential foundation for his lifelong confidence that the gospel is the power of God unto salvation.

context: but he whom God raised again saw no corruption
point_summary: Confidence in the resurrection of Christ — that God raised Jesus and he "saw no corruption" — grounds the missionary proclamation that forgiveness and salvation come through him alone.

context: then Acts chapter 1 records for us the ascension of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ when He ascended to heaven.
point_summary: Acts 1 is cited as the biblical record of Christ's ascension, which the sermon connects to Hebrews 12:2's declaration that He "sat down at the right hand of the throne of God."

context: the Apostle Paul had as he spoke to the Ephesian elders in Acts chapter number 20, and he said this to them, he said that I might finish my course with joy.
point_summary: Finishing the Christian race well matters, as Paul's desire in Acts 20:24—to finish his course with joy—sets the standard for how believers ought to complete the calling given to them.

context: ...but none of these things move me, neither count I my life dear unto myself, so that I might finish my course with joy, and the ministry which I have received of the Lord Jesus to testify the gospel of the grace of God.
point_summary: Paul's declaration that none of life's afflictions move him anchors the sermon's call to finish one's God-given course with joy, counting life itself secondary to completing the ministry received from Christ.

context: Whenever that happens, I immediately go to Acts chapter number 10 and talk about a man by the name of Cornelius.
point_summary: Cornelius in Acts 10 demonstrates that visions alone do not save—God used the vision only to direct Cornelius to Peter, who then preached the gospel through which Cornelius was saved.

context: And if you read the record of Acts chapter number 10, and then as it's rehearsed before the the Jews there in Jerusalem by Peter to them in chapter number 11, you'll find that Peter shows up and Peter preaches the gospel and then Cornelius and all that were gathered there, they get saved.
point_summary: Even when God grants a vision or dream, as with Cornelius in Acts 10, the vision itself does not save—salvation came through Peter's preaching of the gospel.

context: And if you read the record of Acts chapter number 10, and then as it's rehearsed before the the Jews there in Jerusalem by Peter to them in chapter number 11, you'll find that Peter shows up and Peter preaches the gospel and then Cornelius and all that were gathered there, they get saved.
point_summary: Acts 11 is cited as the retelling of Cornelius's conversion to show that even a God-given vision pointed to the preached gospel as the means of salvation, not the vision itself.

context: The disciples in Acts chapter number one, before the ascension, they say, are you going to set up your kingdom?
point_summary: The disciples' question about the kingdom's timing in Acts 1:6 is used to illustrate that God withholds prophetic timetables while still revealing future events progressively through Scripture.

context: It's said this way in Acts four in verse number 20, the disciples say for we cannot but speak the things which we have seen and heard
point_summary: Compelled witness — those who have truly seen and heard Christ cannot remain silent, just as the disciples declared they were unable to stop speaking what they had seen and heard.

context: in Acts 9, he got saved
point_summary: Paul's conversion in Acts 9 is cited as the turning point that transformed a religiously zealous but lost man into a bold gospel witness, contrasting empty religion with saving relationship.

context: in Acts chapter number 20, and we'll close with this tonight
point_summary: Paul's declaration in Acts 20 that he is "pure from the blood of all men" because he did not shrink from proclaiming the whole counsel of God illustrates the watchman's responsibility to warn faithfully.

context: in verse number 24, he says, but none of these things move me, neither count I my life dear unto myself
point_summary: Paul's declaration that he is "pure from the blood of all men" because he faithfully proclaimed the whole counsel of God frames gospel proclamation as a solemn watchman's duty with eternal accountability.

context: There's no other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved
point_summary: Salvation through Christ alone — not works, religion, or any addition to grace — is declared the greatest work God performs and humanity's only means of redemption.

context: Paul had as his own life goal to finish my course with joy, he says that in Acts chapter number 20
point_summary: Paul's stated life goal in Acts 20—to finish his course with joy before the Ephesian church leaders—is here connected directly to his pastoral burden that Timothy, now serving that same Ephesus, would likewise finish strong.

context: Paul says to those same leaders of the church at Ephesus in Acts chapter number 20? He says that he was ministering day and night with tears.
point_summary: Paul's tearful ministry among the Ephesian elders (Acts 20) is cited as precedent for understanding Timothy's own tears while serving that same church amid turmoil, opposition, and false teaching.

context: neither is there salvation in any other. There's only one name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved
point_summary: Salvation's singular pathway—through Christ alone—is the doctrinal anchor, with Acts 4:12's declaration that there is no other name under heaven serving to confirm that every believer's testimony shares the same *how*, however varied the circumstances.

context: Paul is ministering there. And the Bible tells us about his ministry, not only in Ephesus, we find it in verse number 1, that he came to the coast of Ephesus.
point_summary: Acts 19:1 anchors the historical irony that Paul once evangelized all of Asia Minor through Ephesus, making the wholesale defection reported in 2 Timothy 1:15 all the more sobering.

context: We find that the Bible tells us in verse 10 that this continued by the space of two years, so that all they which dwelt in Asia heard the word of the Lord Jesus, both Jews and Greeks.
point_summary: The tragic irony that all Asia heard the word of the Lord through Paul's two-year ministry there (Acts 19:10) makes the wholesale turning away reported in 2 Timothy 1:15 all the more sobering.

context: Peter says to him, he's asking alms, and he says, 'Silver and gold have I none, but such as I have, in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk.'
point_summary: Peter's healing of the lame man in Acts 3:6 is presented as evidence that Peter understood the fulfillment of Isaiah 53's suffering-servant prophecy through Christ's sufferings.

context: And all the people saw him walking and praising God, and they knew that it was he which sat for alms at the beautiful gate of the temple, and they were filled with wonder
point_summary: The healing of the lame man at the Beautiful Gate, witnessed by all the people in Acts 3:9, provides the public, undeniable context from which Peter then preaches Christ's suffering as the fulfillment of Isaiah 53.

context: And as the lame man which was healed held Peter and John, all the people ran together unto them in the porch that is called Solomon's, greatly wandering
point_summary: Peter's use of the healed man's miracle as a platform to declare that God glorified Jesus through suffering before raising Him illustrates the pattern stated in 1 Peter 5:1—suffering precedes glory.

context: And when Peter saw it, he answered unto the people, Ye men of Israel, why marvel ye at this? or why look ye so earnestly on us, as though by our own power or holiness we had made this man to walk?
point_summary: Peter's deflection of personal credit in Acts 3:12 illustrates the principle from 1 Peter 5:1 that suffering precedes glory—the miracle's power belongs to the glorified Christ, not to the apostles' own holiness.

context: The God of Abraham, and of Isaac, and of Jacob, the God of our fathers, hath glorified his Son Jesus
point_summary: Peter's citation of Acts 3:13 in his post-resurrection sermon demonstrates that he understood Christ's glorification as inseparable from—and sequential to—his suffering, confirming the pattern stated in 1 Peter 5:1.

context: 'The God of our fathers hath glorified his son Jesus, whom ye delivered up and denied him in the presence of Pilate when he was determined to let him go.
point_summary: Peter's declaration in Acts 3:13 that God glorified His Son Jesus is set against the reality of His prior suffering, illustrating the sermon's governing principle that glory follows suffering.

context: But ye denied the Holy One and the just, and desired a murderer to be granted unto you,
point_summary: Peter's denial of "the Holy One and the just" in favor of a murderer is cited as evidence that Christ's suffering was a historical reality Peter witnessed and later proclaimed.

context: and killed the Prince of life, whom God hath raised from the dead, whereof we are witnesses.
point_summary: Peter's identification of the crucified Jesus as "the Prince of life" whom God raised from the dead grounds his eyewitness testimony to Christ's sufferings in the resurrection's vindication of that suffering.

context: And his name, through faith in his name, hath made this man strong, whom ye see and know. Yea, the faith which is by him hath given him this perfect soundness in the presence of you all.

context: the power of the Holy Spirit that is mentioned in Acts chapter number 1 and verse number 8

context: And when they heard that notice what it says in verse 24, they lifted up their voice to God with one accord. And said, and here's the recorded prayer, Lord, thou art God. Which has made heaven and earth and the sea and all that in them is.
point_summary: Addressing God as Creator of heaven, earth, and sea grounds the disciples' prayer in reverent worship, modeling how acknowledgment of who God is should precede petition.

context: And now, Lord, behold their threatenings and grant unto thy servants that with all boldness they may speak thy word
point_summary: After grounding their prayer in God's sovereignty over all that had transpired, the early church asked not for relief from threats but for boldness to keep speaking God's word.

context: And when they had prayed, the place was shaken where they were assembled together.

context: And as they're there, the Bible says that there was a man that was begging at the gate, beautiful.

context: They were all filled with the Holy Ghost and they spake the word of God with boldness.
point_summary: The Spirit's answer to the early church's prayer for boldness was immediate and physical—the place shook, they were filled with the Holy Ghost, and they spoke the word of God with boldness.

context: fastening his eyes upon him with John said, look on us.

context: And he gave heed unto them, expecting to receive something of them.

context: And Peter said, silver and gold have I none. But such as I have give I thee in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth. Rise up and walk.
point_summary: The healing of the lame man at the Beautiful Gate—performed in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth—is presented as the notable miracle that set in motion the events prompting the early church's bold prayer in Acts 4.

context: And he took him by the right hand and lifted him up and immediately his feet and ankle bones received strength
point_summary: The healing miracle of Acts 3:7—Peter lifting the lame man so that his feet and ankle bones immediately received strength—is presented as the notable event that prompted the subsequent opposition, arrest, and ultimately the bold prayer of Acts 4:23–31.

context: and he leaping up, stood and walked and entered with them into the temple, walking and leaping and praising God.

context: The Bible says in verse nine and all the people saw him walking and praising God
point_summary: The public visibility of the healed man — seen by all the people walking and praising God — is what made the miracle undeniable and set the stage for the bold preaching that prompted the subsequent opposition and prayer.

context: and they knew it was him notice in verse 10, and they knew that it was him by excuse me, that it was he which sat for alms at the beautiful gate of the temple

context: We find that this notable miracle takes place in Acts chapter number three, and the Bible tells us that there were a great host of people that assembled together, they were greatly wondering, they come to the porch that is called Solomon's Porch, and we find that Peter sees this group of people gathered together and he begins to preach.
point_summary: The healing of the lame man at the Beautiful Gate in Acts 3 served as the notable miracle that drew a crowd to Solomon's Porch, giving Peter the opportunity to preach with boldness and directness, resulting in five thousand men believing.

context: The Bible tells us in verse number four of chapter number four, how be it many of them which heard the word believed. And the number of the men was about five thousand.

context: Peter being filled with the Holy Ghost, he says to them in verse number eight of chapter four, ye rulers of the people and elders of Israel...

context: be it known unto you all, and to all the people of Israel, that by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom ye crucified, whom God raised from the dead, even by him doth this man stand here before you whole.

context: In verse number three, they put them in ward until the next day, put them in hold...

context: we find that he uses that verse that we so often quote. In verse number 12,
point_summary: Salvation is exclusively through the name of Jesus Christ, a truth Peter boldly declared to the very religious leaders who held power over him.

context: 'Neither is there salvation in any other, for there's none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved.'

context: Notice the observation of the religious leaders in verse 13. They saw the boldness of Peter and John and perceived that they were unlearned and ignorant men. They marveled. Notice what it says. They took knowledge of them that they had been with Jesus.

context: Peter and John answered in verse 19 and said unto them, whether it be right in the sight of God to hearken unto you more than unto God, judge ye.

context: For we cannot but speak the things which we have seen and heard.

context: We ought to obey God rather than men.

context: For we cannot but speak the things which we have seen and heard.

context: And when they had prayed, the place was shaken where they were assembled together. They were all filled with the Holy Ghost.

context: And notice the last phrase of verse number 31, when they spake the Word of God, how? With boldness.

context: 'there's no other name under heaven, 'given among men, whereby we must be saved.'

context: 'Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, 'and thou shalt be saved, and thy house.'

context: In verse number 11, 'For we believe that through the grace 'of the Lord Jesus Christ, we shall be saved even as they.'

context: As we look at Acts 27, and I visited this passage of Scripture with the young people on Friday, and I want to develop it maybe a little bit more for us this evening. context: Paul admonished them. Notice what he said to them in verse number 10. He said unto them, Sirs, I perceive that this voyage will be with hurt and much damage, not only of the lading and ship, but also of our lives.

context: Nevertheless, notice verse number 11, nevertheless the centurion believed the master and the owner of the ship more than those things which were spoken by Paul.

context: and now I exhort you to be of good cheer. I know the verse doesn't end there, but can you imagine these guys listening to Paul? Be of good cheer? We just got rid of all of our stuff, right? point_summary: Paul's command to "be of good cheer" amid total despair was not self-generated optimism but a declaration grounded in a direct word from God guaranteeing that no life would be lost.

context: Wherefore sirs be of good cheer. I have it circled in my Bible for I believe God.

context: there's no other name under heaven given among men whereby you must be saved

context: But Peter and John, they answered in v. 19 and said unto them, whether it be right in the sight of God to hearken unto you more than unto God, for we cannot but speak the things which we have seen and heard.
point_summary: Obedience to God supersedes obedience to human authority, as Peter and John refused the religious leaders' command to stop speaking in Jesus' name, declaring they could not but speak what they had seen and heard.

context: And now, Lord, behold their threatenings.
point_summary: Facing serious threats from religious authorities, the early church's response was not to retreat but to pray for greater boldness to keep preaching in Jesus' name.

context: and grant unto thy servants that with all boldness they may speak thy word
point_summary: Facing serious threats from powerful religious authorities, the early church's response in Acts 4:29 was not to seek relief but to pray for boldness to continue preaching Christ's name.

context: V. 17 tells us how they were filled with indignation

context: they laid their hands on the apostles and put them in the common prison
point_summary: Imprisonment by hostile religious authorities could not silence the apostles, as God's direct intervention—freeing them by an angel to return immediately to preaching—demonstrated that divine authority supersedes human opposition.

context: to the people all the words of this life.
point_summary: The angel's command in Acts 5:20 to return immediately to the temple and preach illustrates that divine obedience to proclaim Christ overrides human imprisonment and official threats.

context: And then it says in v. 21, and when they heard that, they entered into the temple early in the morning and taught, but the high priest came and they that were with him and called the council together, the senate of the children of Israel, and sent to the prison to have them brought.

context: But when the officers came and found them not in the prison, they returned and said, the prison truly found we shut with all safety and the keeper standing without before the doors, but when we had opened, we found no man within.

context: And then this one comes in v. 25, it says, then came one and told them, saying, behold, the men whom you have put in prison are standing in the temple and teaching the people.

context: He killed James, the brother of John with the sword.

context: then were the days of unleavened bread. And when he had apprehended him, he put him in prison, delivered him to four quarterians of soldiers to keep him.

context: intending after Easter to bring him forth to the people.
point_summary: Herod's imprisonment of Peter—intended to end in execution—was overturned by God through angelic deliverance, illustrating that the church's suffering does not have the final word.

context: Peter therefore was kept in prison, but prayer was made without ceasing of the church unto God for him.

context: Lord came upon him, and a light shined in the prison. He smote Peter on the side and raised him up.

context: He, Peter, beckoning unto them with the hand to hold their peace, declared unto them how the Lord had brought him out of the prison.

context: And Saul yet breathing out threatenings and slaughter against the disciples of the Lord went unto the high priest

context: as he journeyed he came near Damascus and suddenly there shined round about him a light from heaven
point_summary: The blinding light on the Damascus road marks the precise moment Saul's mounting conviction—resisted through violent persecution—finally broke, resulting in the radical transformation that illustrates 2 Corinthians 5:17.

context: he fell to the earth and heard a voice saying unto him Saul Why persecute us thou me?

context: The Lord said I am Jesus whom thou persecute est It is hard for thee to kick against the pricks
point_summary: The phrase "kicking against the pricks" is read as God's own acknowledgment that Saul had been resisting deep, prolonged conviction—making his Damascus Road encounter the culmination of that struggle and the moment of radical transformation.

context: he was trembling and astonished and said Lord
point_summary: Saul's trembling question — "Lord, what wilt thou have me to do?" — marks the moment conviction yielded to surrender, illustrating the radical, life-altering transformation that defines the new creation of 2 Corinthians 5:17.

context: Look, if you will, in verse number 17 of Acts four, as we make the, set the scene, if you will, for the passage that we're going to look at this morning.
point_summary: Facing an undeniable miracle, the religious leaders resort to threats and commands of silence as their only recourse to stop the gospel's spread.

context: they conferred amongst themselves, according to verse number 15, and they come up with their plan
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: and they call the disciples to them in verse number 18. It says that they called them and commanded them not to speak at all nor teach in the name of Jesus.
point_summary: The religious leaders' command to stop speaking or teaching in Jesus' name sets the scene of direct opposition that the disciples then bring before God in prayer.

context: for we cannot but speak the things which we have seen and heard
point_summary: Compelled by what they had personally witnessed, Peter declares that speaking of Christ was not optional but unavoidable, even under direct command to stop.

context: so when they had further threatened them, they let them go, finding nothing how they might punish them because of the people, for all men glorified God for that which was done
point_summary: Despite repeated threats and a direct command to stop speaking in Jesus' name, the religious leaders were forced to release the apostles because public glorification of God over the healing made punishment politically impossible.

context: the man in Acts chapter number three was about 40 years old on whom this miracle of healing was showed
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: they commanded them in verse number 18
point_summary: The religious leaders' command that the disciples neither speak nor teach in the name of Jesus stands as the direct threat that sets the conflict driving the prayer of Acts 4:23–31.

context: we come to verse number 23 as our passage for our study this morning
point_summary: Upon their release from religious authorities who had threatened and commanded them to stop preaching, the disciples immediately returned to their own company and reported everything, setting the stage for their corporate prayer response.

context: They've already spent one night in prison, according to Acts four at the beginning of the chapter in verse number three.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And now, verse 29, and now, Lord, behold their threatenings. And grant unto thy servants that with all boldness, they may speak thy word by stretching forth thine hand to heal that signs and wonders may be done by the name of thy holy child, Jesus.
point_summary: Facing official threats and commands to stop preaching, the early church's response in Acts 4:29 was to pray not for protection from danger but for boldness to continue speaking God's word.

context: When they had prayed, place was shaken where they were assembled together. They were all filled with the Holy Ghost and they spake the word of God with boldness.
point_summary: When the early church responded to threats by praying together and was filled with the Holy Spirit, they spoke the word of God with boldness—connecting corporate prayer, Spirit-filling, and courageous proclamation as inseparable realities.

context: It tells us in verse 23 that they being let go, they went to their own company.
point_summary: Released from custody, Peter and John immediately returned to their own community and gave a full account of the threats and commands issued by the chief priests and elders.

context: when they heard that in verse number 24, they lifted up their voice to God with one accord.
point_summary: Faced with threats from powerful religious authorities, the disciples' immediate response was unified prayer to God, recognizing that true all-encompassing power and authority belong to God alone, not earthly rulers.

context: In Acts chapter number 4, they say, Lord, behold their threatenings, but God, our request
point_summary: Rather than praying for God to remove the threat, the disciples' request in Acts 4:29 was for boldness to continue speaking God's word in the face of adversity—a response the sermon calls both commendable and exemplary.

context: neither is there salvation any other. For there is no other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved.
point_summary: Salvation belongs exclusively to Jesus Christ, as Peter's declaration in Acts 4:12 — "neither is there salvation in any other" — establishes that no name but His saves anyone.

context: As we look at the disciples here in Acts chapter 4 and we see their response to those that were opposing the message
point_summary: Facing threats and commands to stop preaching, the gathered disciples responded not with retreat but with prayer for boldness, recognizing God as Creator and helper who could sustain their gospel witness amid opposition.

context: ...the opposition didn't stop at the end of Acts chapter 4...
point_summary: The disciples' prayer in Acts 4 was not for opposition to be removed but for boldness to witness through it, showing that God sometimes delivers His people *through* adversity rather than *from* it.

context: ...They call them in Acts chapter 5, and they're like, did not we command you?...
point_summary: In Acts 5, the opposition to the disciples intensified rather than ceased, yet they left beatings rejoicing as worthy to suffer shame for Christ's name, demonstrating God's people empowered through adversity rather than delivered from it.

context: ...That's what they say in Acts chapter 5. Peter, in that passage, he says, we ought to obey God rather than man...
point_summary: In Acts 5, intensified opposition—arrest, threats, and beating—did not silence the disciples but left them rejoicing as ones counted worthy to suffer shame for Christ's name, demonstrating God's people empowered through adversity rather than delivered from it.

context: Thou art God which hast made heaven and earth and the sea and all that in them is.
point_summary: The disciples' prayer in Acts 4:24 deliberately echoes Psalm 146:6, identifying their Helper as the Creator of heaven, earth, and sea—the same God whom the psalmist calls the source of true blessing.

context: In fact, it was in Galatians that they said unless you're circumcised, you can't be saved. And really, we find that that was dealt with if you go back to the book of Acts and you read and you understand the, really the council that took place there in Jerusalem. It's recorded for us, you don't need to turn there, but it's recorded for us in Acts chapter number 15.
point_summary: Acts 15 is cited as the Jerusalem Council's authoritative ruling that physical circumcision is not required for salvation, providing the historical backdrop for Paul's correction of similar errors threatening the Colossian church.

context: to whom also he showed himself alive after his passion, notice this phrase, by many infallible... full proofs, the evidence of His resurrection.
point_summary: Christ's resurrection was publicly verified — shown to many by "many infallible proofs" — demonstrating that his triumph over principalities and powers was an open, undeniable display, not a hidden event.

context: in Acts chapter number 17 now understand in Acts chapter number 17 the Apostle Paul had been faithful preaching
point_summary: Acts 17's account of Paul in Athens is introduced as confirmation that all humanity shares one origin, consistent with the Genesis genealogies tracing every nation back to Noah and ultimately Adam.

context: now while Paul waited for them in Athens his spirit was stirred in him when he saw the city wholly given to idolatry
point_summary: Paul's spirit being stirred at Athens's pervasive idolatry moved him to proclaim the one true Creator God, beginning his declaration from the foundation of Genesis 1.

context: he stood in the midst of Mars Hill and said, Ye men of Athens, I perceive that in all things ye are too superstitious
point_summary: Paul's observation at Mars Hill—that Athens was wholly given to idolatry yet possessed an altar "to the unknown God"—becomes his opening to declare the Creator God of Genesis to a city of false worship.

context: I passed by, and beheld your devotions, I found an altar with this inscription, TO THE UNKNOWN GOD. Whom therefore ye ignorantly worship, him declare I unto you
point_summary: Paul's declaration at the altar "to the unknown God" becomes his launching point to proclaim the Creator of Genesis 1 to a city wholly given to idolatry.

context: things verse 26 and that's made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth and hath determined the times before appointed and the bounds of their habitation
point_summary: Paul's citation of Acts 17:26 in his Athens sermon is presented as apostolic confirmation of the Genesis record of a universal flood and the common descent of all nations from one blood.

context: Peter would declare in the book of Acts as he's standing before the religious leaders, being questioned for the miracle that took place with the healing of a lame man. He says, neither is there salvation in any other. There's no other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved.
point_summary: Salvation is exclusive to Jesus Christ alone — no merit, no other name, no other way to the Father — as Peter's declaration before the religious leaders confirms.

context: But you shall receive power after the Holy Ghost has come upon thee in Acts chapter number one.
point_summary: The power of the Holy Spirit, given at Pentecost, equips believers to fulfill their witness from Jerusalem to the uttermost parts of the earth, as commissioned just before Christ's ascension.

context: And ye shall be witnesses unto me, both in Jerusalem, Judea and on Samaria and unto the uttermost part of the earth
point_summary: Acts 1:8 is cited to ground the Great Commission's outreach in the promised empowerment of the Holy Spirit, with the disciples sent as witnesses from Jerusalem to the uttermost parts of the earth.

context: And he talks about the fact that they would give themselves continually to prayer and to the ministry of the Word.
point_summary: Prayer's effectiveness surpasses human effort, as the disciples' commitment in Acts 6:4 to give themselves "continually to prayer" models prioritizing intercession over self-reliant activity.

context: And he says, brother Saul in verse number 17, the Lord even Jesus that appeared unto thee in the way as thou comest hath sent me that thou mightest receive thy sight and be filled with the Holy Ghost.
point_summary: Acts 9:17 is cited to ground Paul's apostolic call in divine appointment, showing that his ordination as a preacher and teacher of the Gentiles was confirmed through Ananias's Spirit-directed commission to the newly converted Saul.

context: And he says, go thy way in verse 15 and 16 for he is a chosen vessel unto me to bear my name before the Gentiles and kings and the children of Israel
point_summary: God's sovereign choice of Paul as a vessel to bear His name before Gentiles, kings, and Israel undergirds Paul's own apostolic authority and the divine origin of the gospel he preached.

context: for I will show him how great things he must suffer for my namesake.
point_summary: God's sovereign call on Paul included a predetermined path of suffering for Christ's name, establishing the divine appointment that Paul later invoked to authenticate his ministry and instructions.

context: It's an amazing truth in verse number 20 that he preached Christ in the synagogue that he is the Son of God.
point_summary: The dramatic reversal of Saul's conversion is underscored by the immediate fulfillment of his divine calling: the same man who entered synagogues to arrest believers now preached Christ there as the Son of God.

context: We could read that in Acts chapter number 10 and 11
point_summary: Acts 10 is cited as the biblical account of Peter's visit to Cornelius's household, illustrating that Peter's primary apostolic calling was to the Jews (circumcision), in contrast to Paul's calling to the Gentiles.

context: We could read that in Acts chapter number 10 and 11
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Over in Acts chapter 20, and no need to go there in verse 24, Paul said this, that his greatest desire was that he might finish his course with joy.
point_summary: Finishing one's course with joy, as Paul desired, requires staying precisely on the path God has set—even slight deviation leads to wrong destinations and displeasure before God.

context: Sirs, what must I do to be saved?
point_summary: Salvation requires believing on the Lord Jesus Christ first, with baptism following as an act of obedient believers, not as a condition of being saved.

context: Paul would then later say to those that he's speaking to in the book of Acts, how that this Jesus whom I preach is Christ
point_summary: False teaching consistently fails at the identity of Jesus Christ — whether he is merely prophet or good man — versus the apostolic confession that he is the Christ, God in flesh.

context: Paul, in verse number three, gathered a bundle of sticks and laid them on the fire.
point_summary: Acts 28:3 is cited to illustrate that Paul's miraculous survival of a viper's bite authenticated his apostolic message rather than enriching him personally, contrasting true New Testament sign gifts with modern faith healers.

context: Of course, he was the one that in Acts 27, he told them not to go, and they went, and they had that terrible storm.
point_summary: Acts 27 is referenced briefly as background context establishing that Paul warned against the voyage before the shipwreck, setting the scene for the healing miracle in Acts 28 that authenticated his apostolic message.

context: there came a viper out of the heat and fastened onto his hand
point_summary: Paul's miraculous survival of a viper's bite and subsequent healing of Publius's father are presented as sign gifts authenticating the apostolic message, not as normative experiences for believers today.

context: when the barbarians saw the venomous beast hang on his hand, they said among themselves, no doubt this man is a murderer
point_summary: The barbarians' mistaken verdict that Paul was a murderer—reversed only when he survived the viper unharmed—illustrates how the sign gifts authenticated the apostolic message to unbelieving witnesses.

context: he shook off the beast into the fire and felt no harm
point_summary: Paul's miraculous survival of the viper's bite on Melita is presented as a sign gift authenticating his apostolic message, not a model for contemporary faith healing.

context: they looked when he should have swollen or fallen down dead suddenly
point_summary: The miraculous immunity from snakebite and subsequent healing in Acts 28 are presented as authenticating sign gifts that validated the apostolic message, not as normative experiences for believers today.

context: In verse number seven, in the same quarters were possessions of the chief man of the island, whose name was Publius, who received us and lodged us three days courteously.
point_summary: Miraculous healing authenticated Paul's gospel proclamation to the Maltese, a pattern the sermon identifies as recurring throughout Acts to validate the true message of Christ.

context: And it came to pass that the father of Publius lay sick of a fever and of a bloody flux to whom Paul entered in and prayed and laid his hands on him and healed him.
point_summary: Paul's healing of Publius's father in Acts 28:8 is presented as a divine authentication of the gospel message Paul would preach to the people of Melita, not as an end in itself.

context: There were others there in verse number nine and you notice what's taking place.
point_summary: Healing miracles in Acts 28, including verse 9, are presented as divine authentication of the gospel Paul would preach to the people of Melita, not as ends in themselves.

context: ...you should be witnesses unto me, both in Jerusalem and Judea. What is it? Samaria, and under the uttermost part of the earth...
point_summary: Acts 1:8's command to witness in Samaria is read as requiring the disciples to overcome ethnic hatred, with Christ's journey through Samaria in John 4 serving as the preparatory example for that cross-cultural obedience.

context: to whom also He showed Himself alive after His passion. Notice this phraseology, by many infallible proofs, being seen of them forty days and speaking of the things pertaining to the kingdom of God.
point_summary: The forty days of post-resurrection appearances, described in Acts 1:3 as confirmed by "many infallible proofs," are presented as Luke's own corroboration that Christ's eating before the disciples was sufficient evidence of His bodily resurrection.

context: and though they found no cause of death in him, speaking of Jesus the Messiah, yet desired they Pilate that he should be slain.
point_summary: Acts 13:28's placement within Paul's sermon is used to demonstrate that the crucifixion, burial, and resurrection of Jesus fulfilled precisely what was written of Messiah in the Old Testament scriptures.

context: those fellows there in Acts chapter 13, they got a whole lot more than they bargained for
point_summary: Paul's citation of Psalm 2:7 in Acts 13:33 is used to confirm that "this day have I begotten thee" refers to the resurrection, not the incarnation, by comparing Scripture with Scripture.

context: Peter said, neither is there salvation in any other. For there's no other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved.
point_summary: Salvation belongs exclusively to Christ's name — not human works, not self-chosen paths — because Peter declared no other name under heaven can save, and Christ alone paid the required blood sacrifice.

context: but you shall receive power after that the Holy Ghost has come upon you and you shall be witnesses unto me both in Jerusalem and Judea and Samaria and to the uttermost part of the earth
point_summary: The monumental task of preaching repentance and remission of sins to all nations is accomplished not in human strength but in the power of the Holy Spirit, who empowers believers as witnesses to the uttermost parts of the earth.

point_summary: Acts 2:46 is cited as one of several recurring instances of "one accord" in Acts, illustrating the early church's sustained, daily unity in worship and fellowship.

context: 'And when they heard that, 'they lifted up their voice to God with one accord 'and said, Lord, thou art God, 'which hast made heaven and earth and the sea 'and all that in them is.'
point_summary: Acts 4:24 is cited as one of several recurring instances of "one accord" in Acts, illustrating the early church's unified voice in corporate prayer and worship toward God.

context: 'And by the hands of the apostles 'were many signs and wonders wrought among the people, 'and they were all with one accord in Solomon's porch.'
point_summary: Acts 5:12 is cited as one instance in a rapid survey of "one accord" passages in Acts, showing the early church unified in Solomon's Porch amid apostolic signs and wonders.

context: 'Then they cried out with a loud voice 'and stopped their ears and ran upon him with one accord.'

context: 'And the people with one accord'
point_summary: Unity around the Word and miraculous works is shown as the people of Samaria gave unanimous, attentive response to Philip's preaching and the miracles accompanying it.

context: And Herod was highly displeased with them of Tyre and Sidon but they came with one accord to him, and having made Blastus the king's chamberlain, their friend desired peace because their country was nourished by the king's country.

context: 'And when Galileo was the deputy of Achaia, 'the Jews made insurrection with one accord against Paul 'and brought him to the judgment seat.'
point_summary: Acts 18:12 is cited as a negative example—an instance of "one accord" used by enemies of the gospel against Paul, not by the church in unity.

context: 'And the whole city was filled 'with confusion, and having caught Gaius and Aristarchus, 'men of Macedonia, Paul's companions, in travel, 'they rushed with one accord into the theater.'
point_summary: Acts 19:29 is cited as a contrasting example of "one accord" unity among enemies of the gospel, not among believers, illustrating that such unity is not inherently virtuous.

context: therefore, watch and remember, remember what? That by the space of three years, I cease not to warn everyone night and day. You see those next two words with tears, with tears.
