context: Notice in verses 13 through 15, God tells us of what takes place.
point_summary: Divine sovereignty in conversion is highlighted by the detail that the Lord opened Lydia's heart, enabling her to attend to Paul's spoken message—the content of which Acts 16 does not record.

context: Notice in verses 13 through 15, God tells us of what takes place.
point_summary: Lydia's baptism and household faith in verse 15 are noted as evidence that her family was impacted alongside her, though the sermon moves quickly toward the jailer's question.

context: as we note in verse number 15
point_summary: Lydia's conversion and household baptism in verse 15 illustrate that Paul's gospel preaching, though unrecorded here, produced genuine faith and its outward expression in those who heard.

context: ...she attended unto the things which were spoken of Paul...
point_summary: Lydia's attentiveness to Paul's spoken words points to the gospel message he consistently preached—that Christ suffered, rose, and is the Savior—as the content that led to her salvation and baptism.

context: ...Paul, as his manner was, went in unto them, and three Sabbath days reasoned with them out of the Scriptures...
point_summary: Paul's consistent pattern across cities—reasoning from the Scriptures that Christ suffered, died, and rose again—illuminates the gospel content Lydia heard in Philippi, though Acts 16 does not record it explicitly.

context: ...opening and alleging that Christ must needs have suffered and risen again from the...
point_summary: Paul's consistent gospel message—that Christ must suffer, rise from the dead, and is the promised Messiah—is identified as the substance of what Lydia heard and believed in Philippi.

context: the Bible had told us in verse number 16 that she brought her masters much gain by soothsaying
point_summary: The demon-possessed girl's proclamation continued for many days, but her masters' true concern was financial—the loss of profit from her soothsaying, not spiritual truth, drove the accusation against Paul and Silas.

context: It says in verse 19, and when her master saw that the hope of their gains was gone
point_summary: The financial loss from the girl's exorcism—not theological objection—is what drove her masters to drag Paul and Silas before the magistrates, exposing persecution rooted in greed rather than principle.

reference_text: What must I do to be saved?
context: At this particular moment in his life, he wasn't worried about anybody else. He was worried about his own soul's salvation. What must I do to be saved?
point_summary: The jailer's urgent, personal cry — "What must I do to be saved?" — is presented as sincere acknowledgment that he was lost and that Paul, entrusted with the gospel, held the answer.

context: Notice with me the response of Paul as it's given to us in verses 31 and 32.
point_summary: Salvation's answer is simple—believe on the Lord Jesus Christ—costing Jesus everything yet requiring nothing more from the sinner than faith in Him.

context: The Bible says, and they said, believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved, and thy house.
point_summary: Salvation's simplicity is underscored in Paul's answer—"believe on the Lord Jesus Christ"—which offers no list of works but a single, certain promise: "thou shalt be saved."

context: In fact, you can read of his conversion in Acts chapter number nine
point_summary: Acts 9 is cited only as the location of Paul's conversion narrative, which grounds his self-description in 1 Timothy 1:13 as a former blasphemer and persecutor who obtained mercy.

context: Philippian jailer that cries out and says, sirs, what must I do to be saved?
point_summary: The jailer's desperate cry in Acts 16:30 is read as evidence that he recognized his lost condition and knew Paul held the answer to salvation.

context: Paul says, believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved in thy house
point_summary: Paul's answer to the jailer—"believe on the Lord Jesus Christ"—is presented as evidence that Jesus not only came to save sinners generally but desires to save each individual personally.

context: sirs, what must I do to be saved?
point_summary: The jailer's desperate question in Acts 16:30 is treated as evidence that Jesus not only came to save sinners universally but desires to save each individual personally, supported by 1 Timothy 2:4 and 2 Peter 3:9.

context: Peter said this in Acts 4, in verse number 12, he said, neither is there salvation in any other, for there is none other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved.
point_summary: Salvation belongs exclusively to Jesus Christ, whose name alone—identified by context in Acts 4:10—is the one given under heaven through which men must be saved.

context: Notice what Paul said in Acts 16 and verse number 31. He said, believe on the Lord Jesus Christ and thou shalt be saved, and thy house.
point_summary: The certainty of Paul's words "thou shalt be saved" in Acts 16:31 is presented as confident, unconditional assurance—not tentative suggestion—that belief on the Lord Jesus Christ guarantees salvation.

context: In verse 33 and 34 of Acts 16, it says, and he took them the same hour of the night. This is the jailer, and he washed their stripes. Notice what it says, and was baptized he in all his straight way.
point_summary: The Philippian jailer's immediate baptism and rejoicing with his household illustrate that genuine salvation produces swift, visible transformation in the believer's life.

context: When he had brought them into his house, he set meat before them and rejoiced, believing in God with all his house.
point_summary: Salvation's transforming power is seen in the Philippian jailer's immediate response: the same hour he was baptized, brought Paul and Silas into his home, and rejoiced with his whole household.

context: and this man said, sirs, what must I do to be saved?
point_summary: The Philippian jailer's desperate question frames an evangelistic appeal to any unsaved hearer to recognize personal sinfulness and trust Christ as the only Savior.

context: what must I do to be saved?
point_summary: The jailer's question in Acts 16:30 frames the sermon's evangelistic appeal: salvation cannot be earned through church membership, good works, or money, but comes only through the blood of Christ.

context: I thank you for this gospel account in Acts 16, and Lord, as we think of this record that you've provided us with, I thank you for this jailer that got saved.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The Bible says that they looked steadfastly toward heaven as He went up. There were two men that stood by them in white apparel
point_summary: The angelic promise at the Ascension—"this same Jesus…shall so come in like manner"—is presented as confirming and echoing Christ's own earlier pledge to return, offering the disciples concrete encouragement amid grief and uncertainty.

context: you men of Galilee, why stand ye gazing up into heaven? This same Jesus, which has taken up from you into heaven, shall so come in like manner as you have seen Him go into heaven
point_summary: The angelic promise at the Ascension—"this same Jesus…shall so come in like manner"—is presented as divine confirmation of Christ's own pledge to return, a word of encouragement to disciples facing grief and uncertainty.

context: he purchased the church with his own blood
point_summary: The church belongs to Christ as its rightful head because he purchased it with his own blood, making any congregation that displaces his lordship a church of the people rather than his church.

context: they that were scattered abroad went everywhere preaching the word
point_summary: Persecution, far from stamping out the early church, actually scattered believers who then fulfilled the Acts 1:8 commission by preaching the word everywhere they went.

context: the command in Acts 1 and verse 8 was to both in Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and the uttermost
point_summary: Persecution, far from suppressing the church, actually fulfilled the Acts 1:8 mandate by scattering believers into Judea, Samaria, and beyond—proving that opposition advances rather than extinguishes the gospel's reach.

context: I want you to consider what the Bible says in the book of Acts. If you wanna hold your place there in the book of Nehemiah and turn to the book of Acts, if you will. And as we go to the book of Acts in the fifth chapter, and I want you to see what the disciples are doing
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we ought to obey God rather than men
point_summary: When God's work cannot be stopped, even opponents are compelled to acknowledge it—just as the disciples' declaration that they must obey God rather than men preceded a work that Gamaliel himself could not dismiss.

context: we are His witnesses of these things
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: then stood there up one in the council, a Pharisee named Gamaliel
point_summary: Even enemies and skeptics can become unwitting witnesses to God's sovereign hand in a work, as Gamaliel's counsel before the council illustrates the pattern of opposition being forced to reckon with divine activity.

context: And now I say unto you, refrain from these men and let them alone. For this counsel or this work be of men, it will come to naught.
point_summary: Work done for God cannot be overthrown by human opposition, because if it is truly of God, no one can stop it — as Gamaliel's warning in Acts 5:39 declares.

context: But if it be of God, ye can. You cannot, maybe you need to underline that word, circle it. You cannot overthrow it. What's it? The work. If it's of God. Lest happily ye be found even to fight against God.
point_summary: Work done for God cannot be overthrown by human opposition, because if it is truly of God, no force can stop it — a principle applied directly to Nehemiah's wall-building.

context: God is no respecter of persons. Amen. We believe that this evening. The Bible declares that our God is no respecter of persons.
point_summary: God is no respecter of persons, meaning the same Lord who stood with and strengthened Paul is equally available to strengthen believers today in their own labors.

context: He ascended up into heaven
point_summary: Christ's ascension into heaven, referenced in Acts 1:9's account, grounds the claim that Jesus now lives at the Father's right hand, bearing record as one of the three witnesses in unity with the Trinity.

context: 'Sirs, I perceive that this voyage will be with hurt and much damage, not only to the lading in the ship, but also to our lives.'
point_summary: Acts 27:10–11 illustrates that everyone inevitably believes someone, and the centurion's fatal choice to trust the ship's master over Paul warns that believing the wrong witness carries grave consequences.

context: 'Nevertheless, the centurion believed the master and the owner of the ship more than those things which were spoken by Paul.'
point_summary: Acts 27:11 illustrates that everyone inevitably believes someone, and the centurion's fatal choice to trust the shipmaster over Paul warns that believing the wrong witness carries grave consequences.

context: that's why Peter stood up and said, there's no other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved.
point_summary: Eternal life belongs exclusively to the Son, as Peter's declaration in Acts 4:12 confirms that no other name saves—making reception of God's record about Christ the only path to possessing that life.

context: he found this altar that was marked to the unknown God

context: There's no other name under Heaven given among men whereby we must be saved.

context: Peter is the first one. Turn over to the book of Acts in the 10th chapter if you will. He's the first one that God uses to speak to a Gentile man by the name of Cornelius.

context: Then Peter opened his mouth and said of a truth I perceive that God is no respecter of persons.

context: The word which God sent unto the children of Israel, preaching peace by Jesus Christ, he is Lord of all.

context: That word, I say, ye know, which was published throughout all Judea and began from Galilee after the baptism which John preached,

context: 'For they.' Who's they? That's the circumcision, the Jewish people. 'They heard them speak with tongues and magnify God.'

context: 'Can any man forbid water, that these should not be baptized, which have received the Holy Ghost as well as we?'
point_summary: In Acts 10, tongues served as God-given evidence to Jewish witnesses that Gentile believers had genuinely received the Holy Spirit, confirming their salvation before water baptism was administered.

reference_text: Then prayed they him to tarry certain days.
context: Then prayed they him to tarry certain days.
point_summary: Tongues served as God-given evidence to Jewish witnesses that Gentile believers had genuinely received the Holy Spirit, validating their salvation.

context: And the apostles and brethren that were in Judea heard that the Gentiles had also received the word of God.

context: And when Peter was come up to Jerusalem, they that were of the circumcision contended with him.
point_summary: The Jewish believers in Jerusalem contended with Peter over his fellowship with Gentiles, illustrating the cultural and theological barrier that the Gentiles' Spirit-baptism—evidenced by tongues—was breaking down.

reference_text: Thou wentest into men uncircumcised and didst eat with them.
context: Thou wentest into men uncircumcised and didst eat with them.

context: And as I began to speak, the Holy Ghost fell on them as on us at the beginning.
point_summary: Peter's defense before the Jerusalem church uses the Gentiles' reception of the Holy Ghost—identical to what fell on the apostles "at the beginning"—as the observable evidence that God had saved them in the same manner.

context: Then remembered I the word of the Lord, how that he said, John indeed baptized with water, but you shall be baptized with the Holy Ghost.

context: Notice, if you will, in Acts chapter 15. We're going to fast forward a couple of chapters here.

context: when therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small dissension and disputation with them

point_summary: When opposition arose from a sorcerer seeking to turn the deputy from the faith, Paul—filled with the Holy Ghost—confronted it directly, demonstrating that spiritual resistance does not halt the mission of God.

context: In verse number 1, it says, And it came to pass in Iconium.

context: in verse number 50 of Acts 13, it says, But the Jews stirred up the devout and honorable women and the chief men of the city, and raised persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and expelled them out of their coast.

context: And now, as they would be there in Iconium, they would have the opportunity, as God tells us in verse 7, that there they preached the gospel.

context: And the Bible tells us in verse number 3, that they were, look at what it says, Long time therefore abode they, speaking boldly in the Lord.

context: We find that the Bible tells us in verse number 17, Nevertheless, he left not himself without witness, and that he did good, and gave us rain from heaven, and fruitful seasons, filling all our hearts with food and gladness.

context: But notice in verse number 19, And there came thither certain Jews from where?

context: for we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, we shall be saved even as they

context: I wanna draw your attention in the book of Acts to chapter one in verse number 14

context: and they were all scattered abroad throughout the regions of Judea and Samaria, except the apostles

context: James talks about that in Acts chapter 15

context: verse number four of Acts 8, preaching the word

context: Acts chapter eight, they go out and they're preaching the word

context: according to Acts chapter 11, the Bible tells us something that's important
point_summary: Acts 11:19 is cited to show that the scattered Jewish believers initially preached exclusively to Jews, establishing the ethnic limitation that the sermon then contrasts with the gospel's universal scope.

context: In Acts 11 and verse number 19, it says this... now they, which were scattered abroad

context: they traveled as far as Phineas and Cyprus and Antioch, but then notice the end of the verse, preaching the word to none, but unto the Jews only

context: he says it this way in Acts 24 and verse 16, and herein do I exercise myself... to have always a conscience void of offense toward God and toward men
point_summary: Paul's self-examination—exercising himself to maintain a conscience void of offense toward both God and men—is what made external scrutiny by others relatively inconsequential to him.

context: when he saw a city that was wholly given to idolatry, that he was stirred up in his spirit

context: I exercise myself. This was something that he did to have always a conscience void of offense before God and man.

context: Acts chapter number eight

context: he reasoned of righteousness, temperance, and judgment to come

context: Acts chapter number 7

context: they saw his face as it had been an angel

context: But ye shall receive power after that the Holy Ghost has come upon you

context: Acts 18

context: after these things in verse number one, Paul departed from Athens and came to Corinth and found a certain Jew named Aquila born in Pontus lately come from Italy with his wife Priscilla because that Claudius had commanded all Jews to depart from Rome and came unto them

context: he reasoned in the synagogue every Sabbath and persuaded the Jews and the Greeks

context: in previous portion of scripture in chapter 17, the Bible says that he reasoned with them out of the scriptures opening and alleging that Christ must needs have suffered and risen again from the dead and that this Jesus whom I preach unto you is Christ
point_summary: Paul's reasoning in Acts 17 from the Old Testament scriptures—opening and alleging that Christ must have suffered, risen, and is the Messiah—provides the scriptural basis for his synagogue preaching method in Corinth.

context: in verse number five and when Silas and Timotheus were come from Macedonia, Paul was pressed in the spirit and testified to the Jews that Jesus was Christ
point_summary: Paul's arrival of Silas and Timothy from Macedonia coincided with his being pressed in the spirit to testify boldly to the Jews the singular, defining declaration that Jesus is the Christ, the promised Messiah.

context: your blood be upon your own heads, I am clean, from henceforth I will go unto the Gentiles

context: in Acts chapter 20, when Paul says your blood be upon your own heads, I am clean
point_summary: Paul's declaration "your blood be upon your own heads, I am clean" is read through the Ezekiel watchman motif: faithful proclamation transfers moral accountability for rejection from the messenger to the hearers.

context: he departed then and entered into a certain man's house named Justice, one that worshiped God, whose house joined hard to the synagogue and Crispus, the chief ruler of the synagogue, believed on the Lord with all his house

context: then spake the Lord to Paul in the night by a vision. And notice what God tells Paul, be not afraid, but speak and hold not thy peace for I am with thee and no man shall set on thee to hurt thee for I have much people in this city

context: he continued there a year and six months
point_summary: Paul's sustained eighteen-month ministry in Corinth grounds his later claim of apostolic love, demonstrating that genuine ministry requires the investment of significant time with people.

context: we went back to Acts 18 to look at that
point_summary: Paul's 18-month ministry in Corinth, referenced through Acts 18, illustrates that genuine love for a congregation is demonstrated through sustained personal investment of time.

context: as we read in Acts 18, he was a tent maker
point_summary: Paul's tent-making self-sufficiency in Corinth, referenced in Acts 18, is the historical backdrop explaining why he refused financial support from the Corinthians and called that refusal the one way he fell short of other churches.

context: he continued there for a year and six months, teaching the word
point_summary: Paul's year-and-a-half ministry in Corinth, cited from Acts 18:11, is offered as concrete evidence of his love for the church through sustained, costly action rather than mere words.

context: That's what Stephen says in Acts chapter number 7
point_summary: Stephen's citation in Acts 7 that "the God of glory appeared unto our father Abraham" is used to show that God's self-revelation, however partial, was sufficient to produce genuine faith in a pagan context.

context: neither is there salvation in any other. For there's no other name given among men under heaven whereby we must be saved
point_summary: Salvation is exclusive to Christ alone—"neither is there salvation in any other"—because Scripture permits no alternative name by which humanity can be saved.

context: in verse 22 of Acts 17 He says I perceived and in all things you're too superstitious
point_summary: Confronted by a city full of idolatry, Paul's declaration of the unknown God models the believer's calling to seize every opportunity to proclaim who God is to those who do not know Him.

context: For I passed by and beheld your devotions I found an altar with this inscription to the unknown God
point_summary: Paul's declaration of "the unknown God" to Athens illustrates the church's obligation to bring the revealed truth of who God is to those who worship in ignorance.

context: whom therefore ye ignorantly worship him declare I Unto you
point_summary: Paul's declaration of "the unknown God" to Athens illustrates the church's obligation to bring the revealed truth of who God is to a world that worships in ignorance.

context: there's no other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved
point_summary: Acts 4:12's declaration that there is no other name under heaven for salvation is presented as New Testament confirmation of the Old Testament's repeated insistence that beside the Lord there is no Savior.

context: Him declare I unto thee
point_summary: Against the confusion of a world that cannot rightly define God, Scripture's self-revelation of who He is creates both privilege and responsibility for believers to declare Him to those who do not know Him.

context: Notice what Paul said in Acts chapter 20
point_summary: Finishing well spiritually requires purposeful resolve, illustrated by Paul's example in Acts 20:24 as a New Testament parallel to the psalmist's determined, lifelong commitment to obey God's statutes.

context: but none of these things move me. Neither count I my life dear unto myself, so that I might finish my course with joy, and the ministry which I have received of the Lord Jesus, to testify the gospel of the grace of God
point_summary: Paul's resolve to finish his course with joy—regardless of uncertainty or personal cost—frames the sermon's central question of how believers can complete their spiritual lives well.

context: in verse 22, not knowing the things that shall befall me there
point_summary: Paul's willingness to press toward Jerusalem despite not knowing what awaited him there illustrates the uncertainty he embraced in his commitment to finish his course with joy.

context: he says in chapter 21, after they were trying to keep him from going, that I'm ready now to be bound, not to be bound only, but also to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus
point_summary: Paul's willingness to press toward Jerusalem despite Agabus's prophetic warning of imprisonment and death illustrates the resolve required to finish one's course with joy regardless of unknown or foreboding circumstances ahead.

context: as he speaks to these church leaders of Ephesus here in Acts 20, he says that, that I want to finish my course with joy
point_summary: Paul's resolve in Acts 20:24 — to finish his course with joy regardless of unknown dangers ahead — is held up as the defining posture of one committed to completing his God-given ministry at any cost.

context: in Acts 21, in verse 10, it says, and when he was, in verse 10, and as we tarried there
point_summary: Even when the prophet Agabus foretold his bonds and the crowd begged him to turn back, Paul's resolve to finish his course held firm — illustrating that finishing well requires commitment regardless of known cost.

context: many days, there came down from Judea a certain prophet named Agabus
point_summary: The prophet Agabus's binding of Paul's girdle and warning of imprisonment illustrates that Paul pressed on toward Jerusalem despite prophetic forewarning of suffering, modeling the resolve to finish well regardless of unknown costs ahead.

context: he took Paul's girdle, and bound his own hands and feet, and said, Thus saith the Holy Ghost, So shall the Jews at Jerusalem bind the man that owneth this girdle, and shall deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles

context: when he heard these things, both we and they of the place besought him not to go up to Jerusalem
point_summary: Despite not knowing what awaited him in Jerusalem, Paul declared himself ready not only to be bound but to die there for Christ's name, modeling the resolve to finish well regardless of uncertain or costly circumstances.

context: What mean ye to weep and to break mine heart? For I am ready not to be bound only, but also to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus
point_summary: Paul's declaration of readiness to be bound or even die at Jerusalem illustrates the posture of running every day as if it were the last, since no believer knows when his course will end.

context: Paul says that in Acts chapter 20
point_summary: Paul's willingness to be bound or die at Jerusalem, cited from Acts 20 in connection with Acts 21, illustrates the call to live each day as though it may be the last, running the Christian race without assuming more time remains.

context: several years that passed between Paul speaking to those leaders of the church at Ephesus in Acts 20
point_summary: Finishing well requires treating each day as potentially the last, as Paul modeled in Acts 20 by addressing the Ephesian elders before his departure—a pattern later confirmed in 2 Timothy 4.

context: I might finish my course with joy
point_summary: Finishing well is not accidental but deliberate, as Paul's declaration in Acts 20:24—that he purposed to finish his course with joy—demonstrates intentional, daily commitment to the race.

context: David, he's a man after God's own heart is what we read in the book of Acts
point_summary: David's designation as "a man after God's own heart" (Acts 13:22) is invoked to ground his declaration "thou art great" in an intimate, Word-saturated knowledge of God rather than casual familiarity.

context: he saw this altar to the unknown God, Him declare I unto you
point_summary: Paul's encounter with the altar "to the unknown God" in Athens is cited as an illustration of the joy and compulsion to make Christ known that flows from recognizing God's greatness and wondrous works.

context: according to Acts chapter 11, they preached to the Jews only
point_summary: Acts 11 is cited to establish that the Jewish diaspora scattered by Stephen's persecution initially preached only to fellow Jews, forming the audience of Jewish Christians James addresses in his letter.

context: in verse number 15, the Bible says, but the Lord said unto him, go thy way
point_summary: God's sovereign calling of Paul—chosen to bear Christ's name before Gentiles, kings, and Israel—established from the outset that his ministry would be one of appointed trials, not exemption from them.

context: verses 15 and 16 with regard to this fact that trials are to be expected
point_summary: Suffering for Christ is not incidental but divinely foreordained, as God explicitly told Ananias that Paul was chosen and would bear Christ's name—with trials to be expected as part of that calling.

context: go thy way for he, that's Saul, who we know as being Paul, for he is a chosen vessel unto me to bear my name before the Gentiles and kings and the children of Israel
point_summary: God's sovereign call on Paul as a chosen vessel to bear His name before Gentiles, kings, and Israel was inseparable from a divinely ordained path of suffering, established at the moment of his conversion.

context: For I will show him, God says to Ananias, for I will show him, Saul, Paul, how great things he must suffer for my namesake
point_summary: Suffering for Christ's name was not incidental to Paul's calling but was divinely foreordained at his conversion, built into his commission from the very beginning.

context: behold, I go bound in the Spirit unto Jerusalem, not knowing the things that shall befall me there
point_summary: Paul's willingness to go to Jerusalem "bound in the Spirit," not knowing what awaited him, illustrates that suffering is to be expected in the surrendered Christian life.

context: the Holy Ghost witnesseth in every city, saying that bonds and afflictions abide me
point_summary: Forewarned by the Holy Spirit that bonds and afflictions awaited him in every city, Paul's suffering was not accidental but expected—a pattern the sermon presents as normative for surrendered, godly living.

context: That's what Acts 15, that was the debate that was taking place
point_summary: Acts 15 is cited as the backdrop for the Judaizers' false teaching that circumcision is necessary for salvation—the very error Paul refutes by insisting salvation is by grace alone, through Christ alone.

context: that council in Jerusalem that is referenced in Acts chapter 15
point_summary: Acts 15 is cited as the historical backdrop for the Judaizer controversy—the demand that circumcision was necessary for salvation—which Paul flatly rejected as false teaching, insisting salvation is by grace alone through Christ alone.

context: Acts nine, right after he got saved
point_summary: Paul's escape from Damascus in a basket—an episode from Acts 9 occurring immediately after his conversion—is presented as the singular, defining example of infirmity in which he chose to glory rather than boast in strength.

context: Acts chapter number nine and notice with me as we consider what Paul is referencing here when he was there in Damascus
point_summary: Paul's escape from Damascus in a basket—an episode from Acts 9 occurring immediately after his conversion—is presented as the singular, defining example of the infirmity in which he glories, rooting his boast in weakness rather than strength.

context: Acts chapter number seven with the stoning of Stephen
point_summary: Paul's escape from Damascus is traced back to the stoning of Stephen in Acts 7, which the sermon identifies as the event that drove Saul's intensified persecution and ultimately set the stage for his conversion.

context: and desired of him letters to Damascus, to the synagogues that if he found any of this way, whether they were men or women, he might bring them bound unto Jerusalem
point_summary: Acts 9:2 establishes that Paul traveled to Damascus not as a missionary but as a persecutor armed with official letters to imprison believers, making his subsequent conversion and preaching all the more dramatic.

context: Jesus said, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? It is hard for thee to kick against the pricks
point_summary: Christ's identification with His persecuted people—"why persecutest thou me?"—reveals that Saul's assault on believers was an assault on Christ Himself, the moment that arrested and converted him.

context: who art thou, Lord?
point_summary: Saul's Damascus road encounter—going to persecute and returning to preach Christ—establishes the radical transformation that underlies Paul's entire apostolic identity and subsequent sufferings.

context: Lord, what wilt thou have me to do?
point_summary: Conversion's transforming power is illustrated by Paul's Damascus road encounter, where one who journeyed to persecute believers immediately surrendered to Christ's lordship and began preaching Him in the synagogues.

context: but all that heard him were amazed and said, is not this he that destroyed them, which called on this name in Jerusalem and came hither for that intent, that he might bring them bound unto the chief priest
point_summary: Paul's radical transformation from persecutor to preacher left those who knew his original murderous intent utterly astonished, confirming the genuineness and completeness of his conversion.

context: but Saul increased the more in strength and confounded the Jews, which dwelt at Damascus, proving that this is very Christ
point_summary: Saul's dramatic conversion immediately produced bold proclamation that confounded his former allies and provoked deadly opposition, demonstrating that faithful witness to Christ draws suffering.

context: but their laying awake was known of Saul
point_summary: Saul's conversion immediately provoked lethal opposition, as Jewish conspirators watched the city gates day and night to kill him — confirming that faithfulness to Christ draws persecution from the start.

context: in verse 24 at the end, they watched the gates day and night to kill Him
point_summary: Despite open plots against his life—enemies watching the city gates day and night—God miraculously preserved Paul, whose escape in a basket confirmed that no human conspiracy could thwart divine protection.

context: then the disciples took Him by night and let Him down by the wall in a basket
point_summary: Escaping Damascus by being lowered in a basket—an episode of humiliation and vulnerability—is the very example Paul chose to conclude his catalog of sufferings, demonstrating that God's protection came through weakness, not triumph.

context: I had you read Acts 20 and verse 23, 22 and 23 with me earlier, where Paul said that he did not know what would befall him, but he knew that there would be bonds and afflictions
point_summary: Paul's foreknowledge of coming bonds and afflictions, drawn from Acts 20:22–23, sets the stage for verse 24's declaration that none of these things moved him.

context: but none of these things move me. But none of these things move me, neither count I my life dear unto myself
point_summary: Paul's declaration that none of his sufferings moved him, because he counted his life not dear to himself, is held up as the posture that enabled him to endure trial and finish his course with joy.

context: God was gonna use Paul to do what God wanted Paul to do as Paul would surrender and yield himself to God. And God gave to Paul in this first instance, right after he got saved in Acts chapter number nine
point_summary: God's miraculous provision for Paul immediately after his conversion in Acts 9 is presented as the first instance of divine enablement that sustained his entire ministry of endurance.

context: and herein do I exercise myself. Notice what he says, to have always a conscience void of offense toward God and toward men
point_summary: Paul's continual, disciplined striving to maintain a conscience void of offense toward both God and men is held up as the standard of the clean-not-merely-cleaner heart God requires.

context: as Paul says in Acts 24 and verse 16, that I hereby exercise myself to do what? To have always a conscience void of offense toward God and toward men
point_summary: Maintaining a conscience void of offense toward both God and men requires active, ongoing exercise—and being right with God cannot be separated from being right with others.

context: There's no other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved
point_summary: Salvation rests exclusively on Christ's atoning blood, as there is no other name under heaven by which sinners—universally indebted—can be saved.

context: Acts nine is the record that we have, when he's on the road to Damascus
point_summary: Acts 9 is cited as the record of Paul's Damascus Road conversion—establishing that his eternal destiny was settled—so that his aspiration in 2 Corinthians 10 is not heaven but daily faithfulness pleasing to God.

context: And the angel of the Lord spake unto Philip, saying, Arise and go toward the south, and to the way that goeth down from Jerusalem unto Gaza, which is desert
point_summary: Acts 8:26 introduces the divine directive that sets the Ethiopian eunuch narrative in motion, framing the entire passage as a story of how one man came to know Jesus Christ as personal Savior.

context: Then Philip opened his mouth, began at the same Scripture, and preached unto him Jesus
point_summary: Philip's act of opening the Scripture and preaching Christ to the Ethiopian eunuch serves as the sermon's title and central theme, framing the entire message around the proclamation of Jesus from the biblical text.

context: Acts chapter number seven, God records for us the first martyr of the church. Stephen is stoned
point_summary: Acts 7 is cited as the immediate backdrop to Acts 8, establishing that Stephen's martyrdom and Saul's role in it triggered the intensified persecution that makes Acts 8 a transitional chapter in the spread of the gospel.

context: Acts chapter number eight is a transitional chapter here in the book of Acts
point_summary: Acts 8 is introduced as a transitional chapter in which the persecution following Stephen's martyrdom intensifies, setting the stage for the Ethiopian eunuch's conversion and Philip's proclamation of Jesus.

context: The events recorded in Acts chapter number eight are actual events that took place
point_summary: Acts 8:1 marks a transitional intensification of persecution against the early church, which scattered believers and set the stage for the evangelistic events that follow in the chapter.

context: in verse number one, the Bible says that great persecution came, and they were scattered throughout the regions except the apostles
point_summary: The scattering of believers from Jerusalem under great persecution, rather than silencing the gospel, became the very mechanism by which the Great Commission advanced into Judea, Samaria, and beyond.

context: the Bible tells us in verse number four, therefore, they that were scattered abroad went everywhere preaching the word
point_summary: Persecution, intended to silence the church, instead became God's instrument to scatter believers outward, fulfilling the Great Commission as they went everywhere preaching the word.

context: as we begin reading in Acts chapter number eight
point_summary: The persecution that scattered believers from Jerusalem, though intended to silence the gospel, became God's instrument for fulfilling the Great Commission as those scattered went everywhere preaching the word.

context: Jesus said that you're to be witnesses unto me after that the Holy Ghost has come upon you
point_summary: The scattering of believers through persecution fulfilled Christ's Acts 1:8 commission by driving witnesses from Jerusalem into Judea, Samaria, and beyond—turning intended silencing into gospel spread.

context: You're to be witnesses unto me both in Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and the uttermost
point_summary: Persecution that scattered believers out of Jerusalem became the very means by which God fulfilled Christ's commission to carry the gospel to Judea, Samaria, and beyond.

context: the people with one accord gave heed unto those things which Philip spake, hearing and seeing the miracles which he did
point_summary: Persecution-driven scattering produced unified gospel receptivity, as the people of Samaria with one accord heeded Philip's preaching and miracles, resulting in great joy and salvation coming to that city.

context: there was great joy in that city
point_summary: The great joy in Samaria described in Acts 8:8 is identified as the fruit of salvation—people understanding the truth of who Jesus is and what he did for them.

context: The angel of the Lord, according to verse number 26, spake unto Philip
point_summary: Divine redirection in the midst of revival illustrates that sensitivity and obedience to God's voice—even when it means leaving fruitful ministry—marks the servant God uses for specific tasks.

context: he tells Philip in verse 25 or 26, excuse me, unto the way that goeth down from Jerusalem unto Gaza, which is desert
point_summary: Philip's obedience to God's specific, seemingly inconvenient directive—leaving a revival to travel a desert road—illustrates that sensitivity to God's voice must be matched by immediate, unquestioning action.

context: And he arose and went, and behold, a man of Ethiopia, a man of Ethiopia, and eunuch of great authority under Candace, queen of the Ethiopians
point_summary: Philip's immediate obedience to God's directive—arising and going as commanded—is presented as the model response that positioned him to encounter the Ethiopian eunuch.

context: The Bible tells us in verse 27 that as Philip obeys the voice of the Lord, he arose and went, that he finds this man, a man of Ethiopia.
point_summary: God's care for the individual is illustrated by Philip being redirected from a citywide revival to a single searching man returning from Jerusalem for worship.

context: He had gone to Jerusalem, we'd see in verse 27, for the purpose of worship.
point_summary: God's care for the individual is illustrated by Philip being redirected from a citywide revival to a single searching man returning from Jerusalem to worship.

context: We find in verse 29 that the Spirit said unto Philip, go near and join thyself to this chariot.
point_summary: The Spirit's direct command to Philip to approach the Ethiopian's chariot illustrates that God orchestrates individual encounters to bring his word to a single, spiritually confused soul.

context: the Spirit said unto Philip, go near and join thyself to this chariot
point_summary: The Spirit's direct command to Philip in verse 29 illustrates that God cares about reaching the single individual, orchestrating divine appointments to bring his word to one confused, seeking soul.

context: verse 30 through 34 as we begin to see this interaction that Philip has with the eunuch
point_summary: Philip's question — "Understandest thou what thou readest?" — introduces the eunuch's confusion as a man genuinely searching for understanding but unable to grasp Scripture without a guide.

context: how can I? Except some man should guide me
point_summary: Even a sincere, searching person cannot rightly understand Scripture without someone to guide him into its meaning.

context: verse 31. It's a submissive response. It's a response of humility when he says, how can I except some man should guide me
point_summary: The Ethiopian's response — "How can I, except some man should guide me?" — is presented as a model of humility and submission, contrasting the pride that leads people to seek answers in the wrong places.

context: It's amazing the spiritual level that's taking place here in Acts chapter eight and this scene
point_summary: The Ethiopian eunuch's humble response — "How can I, except some man should guide me?" — illustrates that genuine searching for truth is marked by submission, not pride, and that God meets such humility by sending a guide.

context: how can I except some man should guide me
point_summary: The Ethiopian's humble admission — "How can I except some man should guide me?" — is presented as the posture of a sincere seeker who desires truth and is therefore ready to receive it.

context: and the eunuch answered Philip and said, I pray thee, notice his desire to have clarity. He says, I pray thee, of whom speaketh the prophet this? Of himself or of some other man?
point_summary: The eunuch's question — whether Isaiah 53 speaks of the prophet himself or another — illustrates that sincere searching of Scripture, without a guide, leaves even the humble reader without clarity about the identity of the Suffering Servant.

context: Then Philip opened his mouth and began at the same scripture and preached unto him Jesus
point_summary: Starting from the same scripture the eunuch was already reading, Philip's entire message was simply "Jesus," illustrating that the gospel's content and clarity are inseparable from Christ himself.

context: as they went on their way
point_summary: The gospel's clarity is demonstrated in Philip's simple act of preaching Christ from Scripture, with verse 36's arrival at the water shown as the immediate fruit of that message.

context: the vessel in which God used in Acts chapter number eight
point_summary: Philip's readiness to expound Isaiah and preach Christ without hesitation illustrates that effective gospel witness requires prior preparation, since divine appointments cannot be remanufactured.

context: the eunuch is reading and the eunuch says whom speaketh the prophet this of himself or of some other man
point_summary: Philip's preparedness to answer the eunuch's question on the spot—without hesitation—is presented as essential to seizing the unrepeatable divine appointment the encounter represented.

context: Philip here in Acts chapter number eight, when we see the clarity of the gospel that Philip was prepared
point_summary: Philip's pointed gospel witness—beginning from the Ethiopian's own Scripture and preaching Christ—illustrates that effective evangelism stays focused on Jesus rather than being diverted by tangential questions or confusion.

context: Philip opened his mouth and began the same Scripture and preached unto him Jesus
point_summary: Starting from the same Scripture the eunuch was reading, Philip was pointed and purposeful, preaching directly to Jesus Christ — the singular, necessary focus of gospel witness.

context: the eunuch asks of whom speaketh this man of himself or of some other man
point_summary: Philip's pointed, purposeful response to the eunuch's question models the call to steer every conversation toward the cross and preach Christ specifically.

context: Philip is able to, with preparation and with purpose and being pointed, he goes and he preaches unto him Jesus
point_summary: Philip's example in Acts 8:35 is used to call believers to be prepared, purposeful, and pointed in steering conversations toward the cross and the gospel of Jesus Christ.

context: back in Acts 8, the confusion of the eunuch
point_summary: The gospel's power to reconcile sinners to God demands a personal decision, illustrated by the eunuch's conversion as the culminating movement from confusion through clarity to commitment.

context: He preached unto him Jesus
point_summary: Preaching Jesus from the Scripture to someone in confusion leads to a moment of decision, as the eunuch's conversion illustrates that hearing the gospel repeatedly demands a personal response of receiving or rejecting Christ.

context: as they went on their way, they came unto a certain water and the eunuch said, see, here is water. What doth hinder me to be baptized?
point_summary: The eunuch's question about baptism in v. 36 is used to refute baptismal regeneration, affirming that water baptism cannot save but is rather a public identification with Christ following genuine faith.

context: There's water. What doth hinder me to be baptized?
point_summary: The eunuch's question "What doth hinder me to be baptized?" is read as evidence against baptismal regeneration, since Philip's response in verse 37 makes prior belief—not baptism itself—the condition for salvation.

context: I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God
point_summary: Verse 37's confession — "I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God" — is presented as the necessary prerequisite to baptism, refuting baptismal regeneration by showing that saving faith, not the water, is what matters.

context: He answered and said, I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God
point_summary: The eunuch's confession in verse 37 — "I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God" — establishes that personal faith, not baptism itself, is the prerequisite for and basis of salvation.

context: He recognized in verse number 36 his own personal need
point_summary: The eunuch's question "What doth hinder me to be baptized?" is used to refute baptismal regeneration, establishing that baptism follows saving faith rather than producing it.

context: verse 37 is missing from a lot of the modern translations
point_summary: Verse 37's presence in the text is treated as essential because without it, the eunuch's baptism lacks any recorded confession of faith, leaving no basis for distinguishing salvation from the rite itself.

context: he asks the question in verse 36, What doth hinder me to be baptized?
point_summary: The eunuch's question in verse 36 reveals his personal recognition of need for salvation, with baptism properly following only after the confession of faith recorded in verse 37.

context: And when they were come up out of the water, again, that shows us the mode of baptism. It's not sprinkling, it's immersion. They went down into the water, they came up out of the water. The Spirit of the Lord, notice this in verse 39, caught away Philip that the eunuch saw him no more.
point_summary: The eunuch's rejoicing after baptism is connected to the joy of salvation itself — having one's name written in heaven — rather than to the rite of baptism alone.

context: What happened in Samaria in verse 8? There was great joy in that city.
point_summary: The joy the eunuch experienced at conversion (v. 39) is directly linked to the great joy that came to Samaria when the gospel was preached there, establishing rejoicing as the consistent mark of genuine salvation.

context: Acts chapter number eight
point_summary: God's willingness that none should perish, grounded in multiple scriptures, is applied directly to the Ethiopian eunuch's conversion as the pattern and invitation for every hearer's own salvation decision.

context: But none of these things move me, neither count I myself dear unto myself, my life dear unto myself, so that I might finish my course with joy in the ministry which I have received of the Lord Jesus to testify the gospel of the grace of God
point_summary: Finishing the God-given course with joy, not self-preservation, is the resolve that qualifies a servant as meet for the Master's use.

context: You could look back at Acts chapter 18 and we'll reference that in just a few minutes
point_summary: Acts 18 is referenced as the backdrop for Paul's initial arrival in Corinth, where he entered preaching the gospel—defined here as the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ.

context: If you were to read Acts 18 in verses 1 through 11, you can see his entrance into Corinth and how God encouraged him and how he labored there
point_summary: Acts 18:1–11 is cited as background evidence that Paul ministered in Corinth with integrity for eighteen months, grounding his claim in 2 Corinthians 11 to have been "throughly made manifest" among them in all things.

context: he says in verse number 11, and he continued there a year and six months teaching the word of God among them
point_summary: Acts 18:11 is cited as historical evidence that Paul's eighteen months of ministry in Corinth were marked by the transparent, integrity-driven labor he defends in 2 Corinthians 11.

context: after they're being questioned
point_summary: Peter's declaration in Acts 4 that there is no other name under heaven whereby we must be saved is cited to reinforce Paul's insistence that there is no other Jesus, spirit, or gospel.

reference_text: chapter three of the book of Acts
context: that man that was lame, and he sat at the gate beautiful
point_summary: The healing of the lame man at the Gate Beautiful (Acts 3) is cited as the miracle behind Peter's declaration in Acts 4 that salvation comes through no other name than Jesus Christ of Nazareth—directly countering the false apostles' "another Jesus."

context: silver and gold have I not, but such as I have in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk
point_summary: The healing at the Gate Beautiful (Acts 3:6) is cited as evidence that saving and miracle-working power belongs exclusively to Jesus Christ of Nazareth—there is no other name, no other gospel.

context: there's no other name given among men, no other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved
point_summary: Salvation belongs exclusively to Jesus Christ, as Peter's declaration that no other name under heaven saves directly refutes false apostles who preach another Jesus or another gospel.

context: From the Damascus Road on
point_summary: The Damascus Road conversion marks the starting point of Paul's lifelong race of faithfulness, from which he never recanted or trimmed his message until death.

context: In Acts chapter number 13, God the Holy Spirit came and moved and led for Saul and Barnabas to go on this journey
point_summary: Acts 13 provides the historical backdrop for Galatians, recording the Spirit-led missionary journey through which Paul and Barnabas established the four Galatian churches Paul later writes to correct.

context: Acts 13 and 14 record for us all that was accomplished
point_summary: Acts 13 provides the historical anchor for Paul's letter, recording the Spirit-led missionary journey through which the four Galatian churches were founded and later threatened by false teachers.

context: they ordain people to lead the churches at the end of chapter 14 before they return to Antioch
point_summary: Acts 13–14 provides the historical backdrop for the Galatian letter, recording the first missionary journey in which Paul and Barnabas established four churches in the region of Galatia before returning to Antioch.

context: Acts chapter number nine records for us the day in which Paul trusted Christ as his savior
point_summary: Acts 9 is cited as the biblical record of the moment Paul—then Saul—trusted Christ and experienced the life-transforming new birth described in 2 Corinthians 5:17.

context: the Bible says our God has no respecter of persons
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the ship breaks up
point_summary: Even when God does not stop the storm, he sustains his people through the wreckage—losing the ship but not their lives—because their times are in his hands.

context: a snake bite you
point_summary: Even when God permits the storm to break the ship and a snake to bite on shore, his sovereignty over Paul's times means the believer's role is simply to trust, not to dictate the terms of deliverance.

context: the Lord led me back to this text of Scripture in Acts chapter number 6
point_summary: The account in Acts 6–7 is brought to bear as biblical evidence that the people of God are engaged in spiritual warfare, with the enemy's consistent aim being to steal, kill, and destroy.

context: We're going to look at the passage. I'm not trying to look at the passage in Acts 6 and 7
point_summary: The account in Acts 6–7 is presented as biblical evidence that God's people have always been engaged in spiritual warfare, with opposition to prayer, Scripture, and the proclamation of Christ revealing the reality of that conflict.

context: In verse number eight, as this man is being described to us
point_summary: Stephen's character is introduced in Acts 6:8 as "full of faith and power," with faith identified as the foundational quality without which it is impossible to please God.

context: Acts chapter number six and seven
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: but ye shall receive power after that the Holy Ghost has come upon you, and ye shall be witnesses unto me both in Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and the uttermost part of the earth
point_summary: Acts 1:8 is cited to identify the source of Stephen's power as the Holy Spirit, not personal strength, connecting his Spirit-filled character directly to the promised empowerment for witness.

context: this man Stephen was full of the Holy Ghost, and he had power
point_summary: Stephen's being described as "full of faith and power" establishes that his extraordinary ministry derived not from personal ability but from the Holy Spirit, whose indwelling produces both faith and miraculous power.

context: did great wonders and miracles among the people
point_summary: Stephen's power and wisdom, described in Acts 6:8, are attributed not to personal strength or education but entirely to the Holy Ghost working through a man full of faith.

context: then there arose certain of the synagogue, which is called the synagogue of the Libertines, and Cyrenians, and Alexandrians, and of them of Cilicia and of Asia, disputing with Stephen
point_summary: Opposition arising against Stephen from the synagogue is framed as immediate spiritual warfare, with his opponents identified not as personal enemies but as an unsaved mission field resisting the gospel.

context: some of you are already familiar with Acts 6 and 7
point_summary: Stephen's opponents could not refute his Spirit-given wisdom, so they resorted to suborning false witnesses—exposing the conflict as spiritual warfare rather than a legitimate theological dispute.

context: some of you are already familiar with Acts 6 and 7
point_summary: Stephen's opponents could not resist the wisdom and Spirit by which he spoke, demonstrating that his persuasive power was divine, not human—a fulfillment of the promise in James 1:5.

context: verse number 11, which said, we have heard him speak blasphemous words against Moses and against God
point_summary: Because Stephen's opponents could not refute his Spirit-given wisdom, they resorted to suborning men to fabricate charges of blasphemy against Moses and God—exposing the episode as spiritual warfare against an undefeatable witness.

context: In verse 15, the Bible says, and all that sat in the council looking steadfastly on him saw his face as it had been the face of an angel
point_summary: Even under false accusation and the threat of death, Stephen's face shone like an angel's—a visible mark of the Holy Spirit's presence in a man full of faith, power, wisdom, and the Spirit.

context: In chapter seven, in verse number 55, the Bible says, but he, speaking of Stephen, being full of the Holy Ghost, looked up steadfastly into heaven and saw the glory of God and Jesus standing on the right hand of God
point_summary: Stephen's being "full of the Holy Ghost" at the moment of his martyrdom is presented as the source of both his heavenly vision and his Spirit-empowered character throughout his ministry.

context: they stirred up the people and the elders and the scribes and came upon him and caught him and brought him to the council
point_summary: Arrested on fabricated charges of blasphemy and brought before the same council that condemned Christ, Stephen's courage is measured against the known power of that body to destroy him.

context: they set up false witnesses, which said, this man ceaseth not to speak blasphemous words against this holy place and the law, for we've heard him say that this Jesus of Nazareth shall destroy this place and shall change the customs which Moses delivered us
point_summary: False witnesses accused Stephen before the council of blasphemy against the temple and the law, and the sermon highlights this as the context for Stephen's courage in facing a body with the power to condemn.

context: as we begin reading chapter seven, the Bible says, then said the high priest, are these things so?
point_summary: Stephen's response to the high priest's question in Acts 7:1 frames his entire defense as an act of courage—speaking God's truth before hostile, powerful opponents rather than retreating in fear.

context: Much learning hath made thee mad
point_summary: Festus's dismissal of Paul as mad illustrates that even hostile hearers are inwardly pierced by proclaimed truth, confirming that God's Word produces conviction whether met with rejection or openness.

context: thou persuadest me to be a Christian
point_summary: Agrippa's response — "Thou persuadest me to be a Christian" — illustrates that God's Word, simply preached, pierces the heart and produces conviction even in those with great power.

context: when they heard these things, what things? Well, the content of the message, that I didn't take time to read all of it, in verses 2 through 63
point_summary: Conviction from gospel truth, when rejected rather than embraced, can drive hearers to violent rage, as Stephen's accusation of guilt provoked his audience to gnash their teeth and ultimately kill him.

context: he goes and he deals in verse 52, and he goes right to Jesus, which of the prophets have not your fathers persecuted?
point_summary: Stephen's indictment in Acts 7:52 — naming the religious leaders as betrayers and murderers of the Just One — is presented as the convicting truth that drove his hearers to murderous rage rather than repentance.

context: The Bible says when they heard these things in verse 54, they were cut to the heart. And what did they do? And they gnashed on him with their teeth.
point_summary: Conviction from gospel truth provokes varied responses—here, the Sanhedrin's being "cut to the heart" escalates to murderous rage rather than repentance, illustrating that the same piercing word that saves some destroys others.

context: they could not resist the wisdom and the spirit by which he spake
point_summary: The opponents' inability to refute Stephen's message, as noted in Acts 6:10, is presented as the very reason their opposition escalated to murderous violence — truth that cannot be answered is instead silenced by force.

context: he, going back to Stephen being full of the Holy Ghost, looked up steadfastly into heaven and saw the glory of God and Jesus standing on the right hand of God
point_summary: In the face of murderous opposition, Stephen's Spirit-filled vision of the heavens opened and Jesus standing at God's right hand stands in stark contrast to his accusers' deliberate refusal to hear or see the truth.

context: they stopped their ears and ran upon him with one accord and cast him out of the city and stoned him and the witnesses laid down their clothes at the young man's feet whose name was Saul
point_summary: The crowd's violent reaction—stopping their ears and rushing upon Stephen—is presented as the ultimate proof that truth under the Spirit's power convicts even those who refuse to receive it.

context: They could not resist the wisdom and the spirit by which he spake
point_summary: The enemies' inability to resist Stephen's wisdom and the Spirit by which he spoke drove them not to repentance but to violent rejection, revealing that conviction without surrender produces murderous opposition.

context: Stephen that is stoned to death
point_summary: Stephen's death alongside Daniel's deliverance illustrates that God's goodness is not measured by whether His servants are rescued or martyred, but remains constant in every outcome.

context: and they stoned Stephen calling upon God and saying, Lord Jesus receive my spirit
point_summary: Even as Stephen is being stoned to death, his dying words echo Christ's own intercession, pleading that God would not hold this sin against his killers—a final act of compassion the sermon calls its closing point.

context: Lord lay not this sin to their charge. And when he had said this he fell asleep
